AI.tldr: The paper discusses the MLLP-VRAIN group's participation in the IWSLT 2026 Simultaneous Speech Translation task using advanced models and adaptive policies.
AI.motivation: To enhance the performance of simultaneous speech translation systems, particularly in improving quality-latency trade-offs and integrating context-aware mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors used Parakeet and Qwen 3.5 models to build a cascaded system with adaptive 'black-box' policies, incorporating ASR word-boosting and offline pre-translated exemplars for context augmentation.
AI.result: Significant quality improvement of +5.82 XCOMET-XL on the MCIF En→De test set, with an additional +1.03 improvement from context-based processing.
AI.conclusion: The integration of advanced models and innovative context-aware mechanisms delivers notable quality improvements for Simultaneous Speech Translation across multiple language directions.

title: Understanding LLMs in Title-Abstract Screening: From Disagreements to Recommendations
comment: 14 pages + references. Accepted for publication in the 52nd Euromicro Conference on Software Engineering and Advanced Applications (SEAA 2026)
summary: Several studies have examined the use of large language models (LLMs) for title-abstract screening in systematic reviews (SRs), reporting mixed accuracy. However, questions of reliability remain largely unaddressed. In this study, we go beyond quantitative LLM-human agreement metrics and qualitatively investigate how and why LLMs fail. We also propose actionable recommendations. We analyzed disagreements between LLMs and researchers across six software engineering SRs and over 1,000 primary study papers. For each SR, papers were screened independently by human experts and LLMs in zero-shot mode, resulting in Kappa values ranging from 0.52 to 0.77. Qualitative analysis suggests that human-LLM disagreement results from recurring, identifiable causes, such as boundary ambiguity in key terms, keyword overemphasization, and incorrect topic inference. Based on these findings, we propose recommendations such as validating semantic understanding before deployment, running multiple LLMs, and focusing validation efforts on borderline cases. Future studies are needed to validate the impact of our recommendations, and community efforts are needed to develop normative guidelines on LLM usage in SRs.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates why large language models (LLMs) fail in systematic review title-abstract screenings and proposes actionable recommendations to address these issues.
AI.motivation: To analyze the limitations and reliability of LLMs for title-abstract screening in systematic reviews, acknowledging the gaps in understanding the qualitative reasons for LLM errors.
AI.method: The authors evaluated LLM performance in zero-shot mode against human experts across six systematic reviews in software engineering, analyzing disagreements qualitatively and calculating Kappa values (0.52-0.77).
AI.result: The study identified recurring human-LLM disagreements caused by factors like boundary ambiguity, keyword overemphasis, and incorrect topic inference. Kappa values suggest moderate agreement but highlight room for improvement.
AI.conclusion: LLMs show potential for systematic review screenings, but their limitations can be mitigated using recommendations like validating semantic comprehension, using multiple models, and focusing on borderline cases. Further research and guidelines are essential for responsible use.

title: Abstention-Aware Personalized Object Rearrangement via Uncertainty-Guided LLM Assistance
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE 35th International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026)
summary: Robotic assistance in household environments requires not only predicting where objects should be placed, but also reasoning about when objects should not be placed at all. Existing approaches to personalized object rearrangement primarily focus on placement decisions under the assumption of clean observations and complete actionability, limiting their applicability in realistic, cluttered, and partially erroneous settings. In this paper, we introduce APOLLO, a hybrid framework for abstention-aware personalized object rearrangement that combines a lightweight, personalized embedding model (PEM) with selective large language model (LLM) assistance. PEM is trained for each user-environment pair using a small number of demonstrations, operates entirely on CPU, and produces uncertainty estimates, which are used to selectively invoke LLM-based reasoning only for ambiguous decisions, balancing efficiency, privacy, and reasoning capability. To evaluate this formulation beyond existing benchmarks, we introduce APOR, a synthetic, LLM-generated dataset that captures room-level, multi-furniture environments, diverse organizational profiles, explicit abstention behavior, and noisy partial scene context. Extensive experiments on both PARSEC and APOR provide initial evidence that APOLLO improves over prior LLM-based baselines in controlled benchmark settings while substantially reducing LLM usage. Code is available at https://github.com/PaInt-Lab/APOLLO.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces APOLLO, a hybrid framework that improves personalized object rearrangement by combining lightweight personalized embedding models with selective large language model assistance, especially in cluttered and uncertain environments.
AI.motivation: Personalized object rearrangement in household robotics is challenging due to incomplete and noisy observations and the need to balance efficiency, privacy, and reasoning ability.
AI.method: APOLLO uses a personalized embedding model (PEM) to handle individual user-environment preferences and provides uncertainty estimates to selectively engage large language model (LLM) reasoning only for ambiguous cases.
AI.result: Experiments on the synthetic APOR dataset and existing PARSEC benchmark show that APOLLO outperforms previous LLM baselines while significantly reducing LLM reliance.
AI.conclusion: APOLLO balances computational efficiency and reasoning capability for personalized object organization, making it effective in realistic, cluttered, and noisy household environments.

title: Pareto LoRA: Mitigating Modality Imbalance in Unified Multimodal Models via Pareto-Optimal Gradient Integration
summary: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for integrating multimodal understanding and generation within a single autoregressive transformer. However, during multimodal instruction tuning, these models often exhibit pronounced modality imbalance: language gradients dominate optimization, thus leading to lower image generation quality, especially under parameter-efficient fine-tuning such as LoRA. In this work, we systematically analyze modality imbalance in LoRA-based fine-tuning of UMMs for interleaved text-image generation. We show that vision modality performance degrades substantially more than text modality performance when compared to unimodal counterparts, and that modality-specific gradients can differ by orders of magnitude across various tasks and layers. Motivated by this observation, we reformulate the multimodal instruction tuning as a bi-objective optimization problem and propose Pareto LoRA, a Pareto-optimal gradient integration strategy that balances the text and image objectives by modulating the gradient direction and strength. Experiments on the CoMM benchmark with Emu2 demonstrate that Pareto LoRA consistently improves multimodal generation balance, achieving up to 44.9% gains in perceptual image quality over vanilla LoRA while maintaining comparable text performance.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses modality imbalance during multimodal instruction tuning in unified multimodal models (UMMs), proposing a solution called Pareto LoRA to achieve balanced text-image generation.
AI.motivation: Multimodal instruction tuning often suffers from imbalances where language optimization dominates, leading to degraded image generation quality.
AI.method: The study introduces Pareto LoRA, a bi-objective optimization strategy that balances text and image gradients through modulated direction and strength.
AI.result: Pareto LoRA achieves significant improvements in perceptual image quality (up to 44.9%) compared to vanilla LoRA, while maintaining competitive text performance.
AI.conclusion: Pareto LoRA effectively addresses modality imbalance, enhancing the balance between text and image generation in multimodal scenarios.

title: SpeechDx: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Clinical Speech AI
summary: Speech offers a uniquely informative window into health by simultaneously engaging neurological, motor, respiratory, and vocal systems. Current clinical speech AI methods have largely progressed through isolated condition-specific studies, making results difficult to compare and generalization difficult to assess. We introduce SpeechDx, a large-scale benchmark for clinical speech AI spanning 12 datasets and 27 tasks across diverse health conditions. To enable evaluation across shared clinical mechanisms, SpeechDx structures tasks by the stage of speech production they disrupt: conceptualization, formulation, and articulation. The benchmark tests generalization by including tasks with limited labeled data and evaluating the same health condition across multiple datasets, distinguishing clinically meaningful patterns from dataset artefacts. We systematically evaluate 12 state-of-the-art audio encoders across all tasks and under zero-shot cross-condition transfer. Results show that large-scale speech models represent the strongest overall baselines, domain-specific models improve performance only on closely matched tasks, and no current representation generalizes reliably across the clinical speech landscape. SpeechDx establishes a shared evaluation framework for tracking progress toward general-purpose clinical speech representations

AI.tldr: SpeechDx is a large-scale benchmark for clinical speech AI covering 27 tasks, testing generalization and mechanisms of health condition impacts on speech.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the fragmented progress in clinical speech AI by establishing a unified benchmark to facilitate generalization and comparison.
AI.method: The study introduces SpeechDx with 12 datasets and 27 tasks categorized by stages of speech disruption, testing machine learning models for generalization, including zero-shot cross-condition transfer.
AI.result: Evaluation of 12 audio encoders revealed that large-scale models perform best overall, domain-specific models excel in narrowly matched tasks, and current methods lack generalization across the full spectrum of clinical speech.
AI.conclusion: SpeechDx offers a standardized framework to drive advancements in general-purpose clinical speech AI through meaningful evaluation and shared benchmarks.

title: Noise-Driven Escape from Metastable Phases explains Grokking in Deep Neural Networks
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures. Accepted at HiLD 2026: 4th Workshop on High-dimensional Learning Dynamics
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) exhibit first order phase transitions under variations of the L2 regularization strength, with each transition marking the onset of a new learnable feature. Below a critical regularization strength, all features are in principle learnable, but coexisting metastable states, separated by energy barriers, can trap the network and impede convergence. A strength of DNNs is their ability to generalize. But many open questions remain, among them the origin of so called grokking: the abrupt, delayed onset of generalization after prolonged apparent overfitting. We show for linear DNNs that grokking is consistent with hysteresis in first-order L2 phase transitions: using L2 regularization to engineer deliberate trapping, we demonstrate that a model in a low-accuracy metastable state escapes only when SGD noise drives it across an energy barrier, with escape times following Arrhenius scaling. We reproduce grokking-like delayed convergence across two orders of magnitude in escape time by deliberately trapping models in metastable phases. Using sparse sub-sampling we also reproduce the canonical grokking curve where test error eventually approaches the final training error. Our work suggests that the number of metastable states equals the number of learnable features -- one per singular value of the data covariance -- the potential for hysteresis grows naturally with task complexity. We provide evidence that the same mechanism likely operates in general nonlinear DNNs. Our results provide routes toward more efficient learning schemes.

AI.tldr: The paper explores grokking phenomena in DNNs and connects it to metastable states and phase transitions induced by L2 regularization, demonstrating deliberate trapping and escape through SGD noise.
AI.motivation: Understanding the phenomenon of grokking in DNNs, where generalization appears abruptly and delayed post overfitting, and its relation to metastable states in the training process is needed to optimize learning.
AI.method: Through analysis of linear DNNs, the authors linked grokking to hysteresis in L2-induced phase transitions, performing experiments to show deliberate trapping, noise-driven escape times (via Arrhenius scaling), and replicating grokking through sparse sub-sampling.
AI.result: The study confirms that metastable states explain delayed generalization, showing escape times scale with task complexity and the number of features, and validates this mechanism in nonlinear DNNs.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide insights into the emergence of grokking in DNNs due to metastable states and suggest strategies for optimizing learning through better regularization schemes.

title: Tensor-based second-order causal discovery
summary: Causal discovery seeks to uncover the causal dependencies among variables. For this purpose, we propose an algorithm called Tensor-based Second-order Causal Discovery (TSCD). Its input is a tensor obtained from the covariance matrices of observational and interventional data. Assuming the causal dependencies follow a linear structural equation model on a directed acyclic graph (DAG), TSCD outputs the DAG and the functions on its edges, requiring only that the noise variables are uncorrelated. We also implement a version of the approach for nonlinear models. Our focus on second-order statistics (via the covariance matrices) is motivated by their statistical and computational efficiency relative to higher-order moments, their identifiability relative to first-order statistics, and that they work regardless of whether the variables are Gaussian. We show that TSCD has identifiable causal order and parameters from a number of interventions that is logarithmic in the number of variables. Experiments show that TSCD is robust to noise, competitive with existing methods, and scales to hundreds of variables.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Tensor-based Second-order Causal Discovery (TSCD), an algorithm using second-order statistics for efficient causal discovery in both linear and nonlinear models, achieving scalability and robustness.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the limitations in identifying causal dependencies, focusing on statistical and computational efficiency using second-order statistics over conventional higher-order approaches.
AI.method: TSCD transforms covariance matrices into tensors to infer causal graphs (DAGs) and edge functions under linear and nonlinear models, assuming uncorrelated noise variables.
AI.result: The method identifies causal structures with fewer interventions, scales to hundreds of variables, and proves statistical robustness and competitive performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: TSCD is a scalable, effective, and robust solution for causal discovery, leveraging the efficiency and identifiability of second-order statistics.

title: IMPart: Integration of Memetic Operations into Multi-Level Framework for Large-k-Way Hypergraph Partitioning
summary: The problem of k-way hypergraph partitioning is fundamental with significant applications in various fields, including VLSI design and scientific computing. State-of-the-art hypergraph partitioners commonly employ a multi-level framework encompassing coarsening, initial partitioning, uncoarsening, and refinement phases. However, many existing methods do not scale well to problems requiring a large number of partitions (i.e., large k). In pursuit of exceptionally high solution quality, existing memetic approaches often execute their two key operations, recombination and mutation, by invoking separate, standalone multi-level partitioners. This design choice, however, renders them significantly more time-consuming than standard multi-level partitioners. To make such memetic approaches more practical, we propose an advanced memetic framework, IMPart, which introduces novel recombination and mutation operators and integrates them directly into the uncoarsening phase of a single multi-level framework. This transforms the local searches of different granularities in the traditional multi-level framework into a sophisticated, collaborative search. Experimental results on multiple standard benchmarks demonstrate our framework more effectively escapes local optima and explores the global solution space for higher-quality solutions, substantially outperforming all existing hypergraph partitioners for large-$k$-way hypergraph partitioning. Our framework highlights a new paradigm for the development of advanced hypergraph partitioners.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces IMPart, an advanced hypergraph partitioning framework integrating memetic operations directly into the multi-level partitioner to improve scalability and result quality for large-k partitions.
AI.motivation: Current techniques for k-way hypergraph partitioning struggle with scalability for large k and are slow due to reliance on standalone partitioners for memetic operations.
AI.method: The proposed IMPart framework embeds novel recombination and mutation operators directly into the uncoarsening phase of a single multi-level framework, enabling collaborative local searches.
AI.result: Experimental results show IMPart significantly outperforms existing partitioners in achieving higher-quality solutions for large-k-way hypergraph partitioning.
AI.conclusion: IMPart introduces a paradigm shift, making memetic approaches practical and effective for hypergraph partitioning problems with a large number of partitions.

title: AoiZora: Topology-Aware Auto-Parallel Optimization for Inference of Diffusion Transformers
summary: Video diffusion has quickly grown into a key generative serving workload, yet producing each clip demands many denoising iterations over large spatio-temporal latents, which puts low-latency inference out of reach on a single device. A denoising step is therefore typically distributed across multiple accelerators, and TPU sub-slices have become an attractive and practical fabric for doing so. Current auto-parallel systems, however, search almost exclusively over logical device meshes and disregard how a chosen sharding is actually laid out on the physical TPU interconnect -- an oversight that leaves large, topology-dependent performance on the table. We address this gap with AoiZora, a compiler-mediated topology planner built for low-latency video diffusion inference on TPU sub-slices. Its guiding principle is to reconnect logical sharding with physical placement by drawing on different points in the compilation flow: AoiZora first eliminates weak sharding candidates from inexpensive pre-compilation IRs, then compiles only the ones that survive and orders their physical placements using compiled HLO together with a topology-aware communication model. The winning plan is realized along the ordinary compiler path, leaving model code, compiler lowering, collective kernels, and network routing entirely intact. On TPU v5e sub-slices, AoiZora reduces Wan 2.1 one-step denoising latency by as much as 1.42x relative to existing solutions.

AI.tldr: AoiZora enhances video diffusion inference on TPU sub-slices by optimizing sharding placement with a topology-aware compiler approach, achieving significant latency reductions.
AI.motivation: Low-latency inference for video diffusion workloads remains challenging due to computationally intensive denoising operations distributed across multiple accelerators.
AI.method: AoiZora reconnects logical sharding to physical placement by leveraging pre-compilation IRs and a communication model to prune weak candidates and order physical placements for improved TPU performance.
AI.result: AoiZora achieves a 1.42x reduction in latency for Wan 2.1 on TPU v5e sub-slices compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Compiler-mediated topology planning with AoiZora unlocks significant latency optimization for video diffusion tasks without altering the model code or underlying systems.

title: When the Next Step Is Not One Step: Distribution-Aware Execution Modeling for Concurrent Go Programs
summary: Training a model to predict the next step in a concurrent program is harder than it looks: two runs of the same program from the same trace prefix can produce different next events, both valid, because the scheduler is nondeterministic. A model trained against a single label is learning to guess one outcome of a random process. We turn this around and use the nondeterminism as a training signal. We run each program many times, aggregate the observed next events into an empirical distribution, and fine-tune a 7B model to match that distribution with a KL objective. On 798 held-out predictions drawn from real production Go bugs (CockroachDB, Kubernetes, gRPC, etcd), fine-tuning on fewer than a thousand traces reaches 36.2% accuracy, ahead of Gemini 3.5 Flash used zero-shot (34.8%) and the same model without fine-tuning (28.6%). Distribution training matches cross-entropy on accuracy (35.8% vs. 36.2%) while reducing Expected Calibration Error from 0.205 to 0.169. We also derive a formal goroutine-leak signature for a class of select-blocked goroutines where P(GoUnblock)=0 holds by scheduler semantics, not by learning. We release the dataset, trained adapters, and all tooling.

AI.tldr: The paper explores a novel approach for training models to predict the next step in concurrent programs by leveraging nondeterminism in scheduling as a training signal.
AI.motivation: Predicting the next step in a concurrent program is challenging due to the nondeterminism introduced by decision schedulers, as traditional methods train a model for a single, random outcome. This paper seeks to utilize nondeterminism to improve model predictions.
AI.method: The authors propose running multiple instances of the same program to aggregate empirical distributions of next events and fine-tune a 7B parameter model using a Kullback-Leibler (KL) objective.
AI.result: The fine-tuned model achieves 36.2% accuracy on a dataset of real-world production bugs, outperforming both zero-shot approaches and models without fine-tuning. Distribution training also improved calibration, lowering the Expected Calibration Error from 0.205 to 0.169.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that leveraging nondeterminism as a training signal can improve both prediction accuracy and calibration in the context of concurrent programming. Additionally, the authors offer a formal categorization for goroutine-leak scenarios and provide publicly available resources for replication and testing.

title: Are you speaking my languages? On spoken language adherence in multimodal LLMs
comment: 7 pages, 3 tables in the main body
summary: While Large Language Model (LLM) based Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) enables seamless multilingual use, models often misidentify the output language, compromising transcription fidelity and downstream application quality. To preserve flexibility and code-switching capabilities, we propose a soft prompting approach that hints at potential spoken languages without strictly constraining the output. We formally define this challenge as a lack of language adherence, introduce a novel metric to quantify violations, and evaluate three mitigation strategies: (1) zero-shot prompting for robust guidance under uncertainty, (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to improve prompt adherence, and (3) Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to enforce adherence during decoding. We present a comparative analysis of these methods across multiple languages, evaluating effectiveness in reducing the language violation while maintaining overall ASR performance. Finally, we discuss trade-offs to guide strategy selection under various compute constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses language misidentification in multilingual ASR models by proposing soft prompting and evaluates three strategies to improve language adherence without sacrificing ASR performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the issue in multilingual ASR systems where language misidentification compromises transcription accuracy and application quality, while preserving flexibility and code-switching capabilities.
AI.method: The proposed method includes a soft prompting approach, defining the problem as language adherence, introducing a metric, and evaluating three strategies: zero-shot prompting, supervised fine-tuning, and Chain-of-Thought reasoning.
AI.result: The study compares the three strategies across languages and demonstrates their effectiveness in reducing language violation while maintaining ASR performance.
AI.conclusion: Key trade-offs and guidance are discussed for selecting appropriate strategies under different computational constraints, balancing language adherence and ASR performance.

title: Why Model Credibility Isn't Enough: -Rethinking Trust in Simulation Architectures
comment: Annual Congress of Japan Society of Automotive Engineers (JSAE), May 2026, Yokohama, Japan
summary: Credibility of a simulation model is an important topic. Several approaches try to quantify the credibility of simulation. However, models are mostly assembled within a simulation architecture. Can the credibility of a simulation architecture be assessed based on the credibility of the models that comprise it? This paper aims to address this issue by providing an overview of the current state of the art in the field of assembly credibility. It will compare sensitivity analysis techniques, qualitative analysis by experts, explainability in AI, and networks. Finally, an assessment of the proposed approaches, based on criteria such as rigor, generalization, and resource requirements, will reveal the strengths and weaknesses of each approach.

AI.tldr: The paper explores whether the credibility of a simulation architecture can be assessed from its constituent models, comparing multiple techniques for evaluation.
AI.motivation: To address if simulation architecture credibility can be determined based on the credibility of individual models and to evaluate current approaches for assembly credibility.
AI.method: The study provides a comparative analysis of sensitivity techniques, expert qualitative review, AI explainability, and networks while assessing these approaches based on rigor, generalization, and resource requirements.
AI.result: The paper reveals the strengths and weaknesses of the discussed techniques in the context of simulation architecture credibility.
AI.conclusion: Understanding and improving simulation architecture credibility is a complex task, and the analyzed approaches provide various trade-offs in terms of application and utility.

title: Transformer-Based Warm-Starting for Feasible and Optimal Terminal Approach to Tumbling Objects with Space Manipulators
summary: Real-time trajectory generation for on-orbit robotic servicing is challenging due to the nonlinear coupling between spacecraft bus motion, manipulator dynamics, visibility cone, and trajectory-level safety constraints. This paper studies learning-based warm-starting for sequential convex programming (SCP) in the terminal approach of a space manipulator toward a tumbling target. The proposed framework decomposes the problem into a system center-of-mass translational planning stage and a coupled attitude--manipulator torque-allocation stage, and applies a causal transformer warm-start to the latter, which constitutes the dominant computational bottleneck. Linear and flow matching action decoders are compared under different action-chunking and training dataset sizes, and the resulting warm-starts are evaluated under both cost-optimal and feasibility projection using SCP. Across 300 held-out scenarios, the learned warm-start reduces the second-stage SCP iteration count by up to 28% and the runtime by 23% while preserving the final control-cost distribution. When the learned warm-starts are used for nonconvex feasibility projection, they nearly halve the runtime relative to cost-optimal SCP, while avoiding the catastrophic high-cost tail behavior observed when initialized heuristically. These results indicate that sequence-model warm-starts can improve both the computational efficiency and trajectory robustness of optimization-based terminal guidance for space manipulation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a learning-based warm-start method using a causal transformer to improve real-time trajectory generation for on-orbit robotic servicing, showing significant runtime and efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: Real-time trajectory generation in space manipulation is computationally challenging due to complex nonlinear dynamics, safety constraints, and the need for robust, efficient optimization solutions.
AI.method: The problem is decomposed into two stages: center-of-mass translational planning and coupled attitude-manipulator torque allocation. A causal transformer is employed to warm-start the computationally expensive torque-allocation stage. Different action-decoding strategies and their impact on SCP iteration count and runtime are tested.
AI.result: Learning-based warm-starts using causal transformers reduced the SCP iteration count by up to 28% and runtime by 23% under optimal scenarios. They also halved the runtime under nonconvex feasibility projection while avoiding high-cost outcomes associated with heuristic approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed sequence-model warm-start framework enhances the computational efficiency and trajectory reliability of optimization-based terminal guidance for space manipulator operations.

title: Reasoning Text-to-Video Retrieval for Operating Room Clips via Action-Driven Digital Twins
summary: Text-to-video retrieval in operating rooms (OR) is an enabling technology for OR safety, as it allows stakeholders to retrieve and inspect recordings of specific events. However, because the most safety-critical events may not follow the common structure, to unlock its full potential text-to-video retrieval must be able to handle implicit queries that require reasoning to identify the right video (e.g., the step right before clipping). However, existing methods rely on global embeddings that cannot reason over such queries. We propose OR3, a text-to-video retrieval method that converts clips into action-driven digital twins (ActDTs), grouping concurrent subject-action-object triplets under non-overlapping temporal intervals. Moreover, rather than cross-modal matching through paired encoders, OR3 performs imagination-based retrieval where an LLM generates hypothetical ActDTs from queries. This enables intra-modal matching via a single encoder trained with ActDT-tailored hard negatives. Finally, evidence-grounded refinement revises imagined ActDTs based on discrepancies with top candidates to capture procedure-specific patterns. We construct a benchmark from MM-OR with 276 implicit queries across four reasoning categories over 386 clips from robotic knee procedures. OR3 achieves 57.6 R@1 and 77.3 R@5, outperforming the strongest baseline. These results demonstrate that OR3 enables fine-grained discrimination between visually similar OR video clips through temporal action reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OR3, a novel text-to-video retrieval method for operating rooms (ORs), which enhances performance by using action-driven reasoning instead of global embeddings.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of current text-to-video retrieval methods in operating rooms, which struggle to handle implicit queries requiring reasoning for safety-critical events.
AI.method: OR3 uses Action-driven Digital Twins (ActDTs), intra-modal matching via imagined ActDTs generated by a large language model, and evidence-grounded refinement to improve retrieval accuracy.
AI.result: OR3 achieved significant performance with 57.6 R@1 and 77.3 R@5 on a benchmark with 386 robotic knee procedure clips, surpassing existing methods.
AI.conclusion: OR3 demonstrates superior fine-grained temporal action reasoning, allowing effective retrieval of visually similar OR video clips needed for safety-critical applications.

title: Distributed General-Purpose Agent Networks: Architecture, Key Mechanisms, and Prototypes
summary: Large language models have accelerated the transition from passive conversational assistants to autonomous agents that can understand goals, plan actions, invoke tools, and execute multi-step tasks. Yet the capability of a single agent remains constrained by its local data, tool permissions, runtime environment, and governance boundary. This paper studies distributed general-purpose agent networks: open peer-to-peer networks in which heterogeneous agents deployed on personal devices, edge nodes, or autonomous computing environments can discover one another, establish trust, negotiate cooperation rules, and execute open-ended tasks. We argue that such networks cannot be obtained by simply combining existing peer-to-peer overlays with conventional multi-agent systems. Unlike traditional P2P networks, agent networks must propagate semantic declarations about intentions, capabilities, states, and cooperation constraints. We therefore propose a layered architecture centered on a protocol adaptation layer that connects upper-level task semantics with lower-level network operations. Based on this architecture, the paper identifies three core mechanism problems: semantic announcement propagation for collaborator discovery, verifiable identity and multi-topic reputation for cooperation governance, and semantic-gradient mechanism design for open task execution. For each problem, we present a technical route, including bodyless gossip with sequential logs, BAID-based identity binding with MG-EigenTrust reputation, and a Stackelberg-style mechanism-generation loop driven by semantic attribution feedback. We further report prototype overhead results for BAID-style tiered verification and mechanism-level simulations of MG-EigenTrust under cross-topic disguise-collusion attacks. The resulting framework provides a system-level foundation for open, trustworthy, and scalable agent collaboration.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework for distributed agent networks by identifying architectural and operational challenges and solving them with innovative mechanisms.
AI.motivation: This work is motivated by the need for autonomous agents to collaborate across boundaries in open peer-to-peer networks, overcoming the limitations of single-agent systems.
AI.method: The authors propose a layered architecture and tackle three key challenges: semantic announcement propagation, identity verification with multi-topic reputation, and mechanism design for tasks. Technical methods include bodyless gossip, BAID-based identity binding, and semantic-gradient-based design.
AI.result: Prototype experiments are conducted for tiered BAID verification and MG-EigenTrust simulations, showing promise against challenges like collusion and cross-topic issues.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework lays a foundation for scalable, collaborative, and trustworthy agent networks capable of open-ended task executions.

title: Towards Fast GNN Surrogates for CO2 Migration in Complex Geological Formations
summary: This chapter discusses how a data-driven machine learning approach can reproduce key aspects of the physical behavior of multiphase flows in complex geological formations. We propose an end-to-end graph neural surrogate tailored to CO$_2$ plume migration forecasting in geological storage. The method is evaluated on the SPE11A benchmark, a well-known industry test case designed to assess CO$_2$ storage scenarios and characterized by sharp gas-water interfaces, strong advective transport, and rapid convective mixing with fingering development. The benchmark is reformulated as a graph in which nodes represent computational cells and edges encode transmissibility-based interactions enriched with geometric attributes. Directional transport arising from grid geometry, permeability contrasts, and geological heterogeneity is captured through an anisotropic message-passing mechanism, where interaction weights are computed via geometry-conditioned edge embeddings, biasing message aggregation toward physically relevant transport directions. Temporal evolution is modeled in latent space using an autoregressive residual formulation trained with multi-step supervision. The proposed model produces competitive forecasts of gas saturation and liquid-phase density, which are key indicators for CO$_2$ storage monitoring, with cumulative errors that remain moderate over extended forecasting horizons.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a graph neural network for forecasting CO₂ plume migration in geological storage, evaluated using the SPE11A benchmark. Results show competitive predictions over time.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for accurate and efficient modeling of multiphase flows in geological formations, particularly in the context of CO₂ storage monitoring, which is challenging due to complex dynamics like advective transport and convective mixing.
AI.method: The proposed method uses an end-to-end graph neural network on a graph representation of the SPE11A benchmark. Computational cells are treated as nodes, and geometric and transmissibility attributes are incorporated into edges. An anisotropic message-passing mechanism and autoregressive time modeling are used.
AI.result: The model demonstrates competitive forecasting performance for gas saturation and liquid-phase density, with reduced cumulative errors over time, making it effective for CO₂ storage monitoring needs.
AI.conclusion: The graph neural network surrogate effectively reproduces key physical behaviors of multiphase flows in complex geological structures, offering a promising tool for reliable, long-horizon forecasting in CO₂ storage scenarios.

title: A Diffusion Approximation for Temporal-Difference Learning with Linear Features under Markovian Noise
summary: Temporal difference (TD) learning with linear function approximation is a core method for policy evaluation. Its classical continuous-time description is an ordinary differential equation (ODE), which captures the asymptotic mean dynamics but neglects stochastic fluctuations determining the error floor. We introduce a stochastic differential equation (SDE) approximation for linear TD(0) under Markovian noise. The resulting model distinguishes the contraction dynamics governed by the projected Bellman operator from the influence of Markovian sampling. As a consequence, the model explains the constant-stepsize error floor through the interaction between Markovian long-run covariance and the contraction geometry of the projected Bellman operator.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a stochastic differential equation (SDE) model for linear TD(0) learning, offering insights into the constant-stepsize error floor by analyzing Markovian noise and contraction geometry.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of the classical ordinary differential equation (ODE) description of temporal difference (TD) learning, which neglects stochastic fluctuations and the related error floor.
AI.method: The authors construct a stochastic differential equation (SDE) approximation for linear TD(0) learning under Markovian noise, separating the contraction dynamics from the effects of Markovian sampling.
AI.result: The SDE model effectively distinguishes between the contraction dynamics of the projected Bellman operator and the impact of Markovian long-run covariance, explaining the behavior of the constant-stepsize error floor.
AI.conclusion: The introduction of an SDE framework clarifies the interaction between Markovian sampling and contraction dynamics, providing a more comprehensive understanding of error behavior in TD learning.

title: ComPart: Community-Guided Post-Coarsening for High-Quality Hypergraph Partitioning
summary: Hypergraph partitioning is a critical step in the design of complex embedded systems, essential for optimizing task mapping on heterogeneous MPSoCs and enabling multi-FPGA prototyping. Many existing methods rely on community detection to identify modules with dense internal and sparse external connections, typically utilizing them to constrain the coarsening phase--a widely adopted paradigm. In this work, we propose ComPart, a generalized framework that integrates diverse community detection methods to uncover high-quality clusterings throughout the post-coarsening stages (i.e., initial partitioning and uncoarsening). These discovered clusterings serve as distinct structural guides, enabling the refinement process to identify superior partitioning solutions. Our framework offers two key advantages: (1) it establishes a new paradigm that leverages community structures detected during uncoarsening to escape local optima and explore globally meaningful solution subspaces, transcending the limitations of standard local refinements; and (2) it flexibly accommodates both existing and future community detection methods. Furthermore, we theoretically generalize locally-dense decomposition--originally from graphs--to the hypergraph domain. We provide the formal extension and necessary proofs to apply this technique to hypergraphs, marking its first application in hypergraph partitioning. Specifically, we utilize this rigorously derived decomposition to guide the initial partitioning phase toward superior starting points. Experimental results on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in solution quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ComPart, a framework for hypergraph partitioning that integrates community detection in all phases, providing better partitioning solutions and outperforming current methods.
AI.motivation: Hypergraph partitioning is essential for task mapping in heterogeneous MPSoCs and multi-FPGA prototyping. However, existing methods are restricted to community detection primarily during coarsening, limiting optimization capabilities.
AI.method: ComPart builds on generalized community detection methods applied throughout the partitioning phases, including uncoarsening. It formalizes locally-dense decomposition for hypergraphs, using it to guide initial partitioning and refinement.
AI.result: Experimental tests on benchmarks show that ComPart delivers consistently superior partitioning quality compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: ComPart's integration of community detection enables exploration of globally meaningful solutions and avoids local optima, presenting a flexible framework adaptable to future community detection advancements.

title: From GPU to Microcontroller: Online Ridge Regression for Edge-Deployable Traffic Prediction
comment: 8 pages, IEEE Intelligent Transportation Systems Conference, 2026
summary: State-of-the-art traffic flow forecasting models, including Graph Convolutional Networks and graph-less MLPs, require centralized GPU training across all sensors, making them impractical for resource-constrained intelligent transportation deployments. We show that much of this complexity is unnecessary. A parametric analysis of the recent graph-less model GLMST reveals that reducing its internal embedding dimension from 64 to 4 degrades MAPE by less than one percentage point, suggesting that the model's effective capacity far exceeds what the task requires. Motivated by this finding, we replace the neural architecture entirely with per-sensor Ridge regression using horizon-aligned periodic features, combined with Recursive Least Squares (RLS) for online adaptation. With only 444 parameters per sensor (80x fewer than GLMST) and test-time online adaptation, our method achieves the best MAPE on three of four PEMS benchmarks, and remains within one percentage point on the fourth. Because each sensor's model is self-contained and involves only elementary linear algebra, the entire pipeline (training, inference, and online adaptation) runs on edge hardware without a GPU. An ESP32 microcontroller (160 MHz, 520 KB SRAM) completes cold-start training in 7.4s and each predict-and-update in under 2ms with zero heap allocation; a single Raspberry Pi 5 core completes cold-start training in 0.21s and each predict-and-update in 0.26ms.

AI.tldr: State-of-the-art traffic flow forecasting models require centralized GPU training, but this paper proposes a simpler method using per-sensor Ridge regression with online adaptation, outperforming benchmarks with significantly reduced resource requirements.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a lightweight, resource-efficient model that eliminates the need for centralized GPU-based training and inference, making it practical for intelligent transportation systems in resource-constrained environments.
AI.method: The paper uses a novel approach, replacing complex neural architectures with per-sensor Ridge regression combined with horizon-aligned periodic features and Recursive Least Squares (RLS) for online adaptation.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves the best performance (MAPE) on three of four PEMS benchmarks and remains competitive on the fourth. It dramatically reduces parameter counts (80x fewer than GLMST), enabling real-time training and inference on resource-limited edge devices.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that simpler per-sensor models with efficient online adaptation can outperform or match state-of-the-art methods while being practical for edge-based deployments, highlighting an exciting direction for lightweight traffic forecasting systems.

title: Bridging Functional Correctness and Runtime Efficiency Gaps in LLM-Based Code Translation
summary: While large language models (LLMs) have greatly advanced the functional correctness of automated code translation systems, the runtime efficiency of translated programs has received comparatively little attention. With the waning of Moore's law, runtime efficiency has become increasingly important for program quality, alongside functional correctness. Our preliminary study reveals that LLM-translated programs often run slower than human-written ones, and this issue cannot be remedied through prompt engineering alone. Therefore, our work proposes SwiftTrans, a code translation framework comprising two key stages: (1) Multi-Perspective Exploration, where MpTranslator leverages parallel in-context learning (ICL) to generate diverse translation candidates; and (2) Difference-Aware Selection, where DiffSelector identifies the optimal candidate by explicitly comparing differences between translations. We further introduce Hierarchical Guidance for MpTranslator and Ordinal Guidance for DiffSelector, enabling LLMs to better adapt to these two core components. To support the evaluation of runtime efficiency in translated programs, we extend existing benchmarks, CodeNet and F2SBench, and introduce a new benchmark, SwiftBench. Experimental results across all three benchmarks show that SwiftTrans achieves consistent improvements in both correctness and runtime efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SwiftTrans, a framework for improving runtime efficiency and correctness in code translations generated by large language models (LLMs). It comprises two stages: diverse candidate generation and optimal selection through comparison.
AI.motivation: Runtime efficiency in code translation, alongside functional correctness, is increasingly crucial as LLM-generated code often runs slower than human-written code. Current methods fail to address this problem effectively.
AI.method: The authors propose SwiftTrans, a two-stage framework. Stage 1 uses Multi-Perspective Exploration with parallel in-context learning to produce diverse translations. Stage 2 applies Difference-Aware Selection to choose the best translation based on explicit comparison. Enhancements include Hierarchical and Ordinal Guidance mechanisms.
AI.result: SwiftTrans improves both runtime efficiency and correctness of translated programs, as demonstrated across extended and new benchmarks (CodeNet, F2SBench, and SwiftBench).
AI.conclusion: SwiftTrans is effective in addressing runtime inefficiencies in LLM-translated code by combining diverse candidate generation and targeted selection, marking a significant advance in automated code translation.

title: Examining the Limits of Word2Vec with Toki Pona
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted to the Society for Computation in Linguistics (SCiL) 2026
summary: Word2Vec's effectiveness at generating semantic embeddings has been widely validated, yet it has been tested almost exclusively on languages with large vocabulary inventories. This study examines whether Word2Vec can successfully capture semantic relationships within an extremely reduced vocabulary using data from Toki Pona, a constructed language with approximately 130 words. We sourced 1.4 million sentences (7.95 million tokens) from the Toki Pona community for training. Approximately 23% of sentences in the corpus contain non-Toki Pona tokens such as named entities, loanwords, and neologisms. To investigate whether this linguistic noise enhances or hinders performance -- a topic rarely addressed in word embedding literature -- we trained two distinct models: one retaining these incidental tokens and another filtering them out completely. Evaluation was conducted using quantitative methods measuring word proximity to semantic category centroids, automated silhouette scores via agglomerative clustering, and qualitative analysis utilizing representational similarity matrices compared against English. The results indicate that while sparse, non-core tokens do not affect the relative structure of the learned embeddings, they actually draw similar words closer together in the vector space. Importantly, Word2Vec's effectiveness depends more on distributional patterns than lexicon size even at this extreme lower bound.

AI.tldr: This study tests Word2Vec's ability to capture semantic relationships in Toki Pona, a language with just ~130 words, and finds distributional patterns matter more than lexicon size.
AI.motivation: To assess whether Word2Vec can effectively generate semantic embeddings in a minimal vocabulary context and whether linguistic noise impacts performance.
AI.method: The paper trains two models with 1.4M sentences from Toki Pona: one preserving non-core tokens and another filtering them. Various quantitative and qualitative evaluation methods are used.
AI.result: Sparse non-core tokens do not alter embedding structures but enhance vector proximity for similar words. Distributional patterns override lexical size.
AI.conclusion: Word2Vec effectively captures semantic relationships in small lexicons, with linguistic noise contributing positively rather than hindering embeddings.

title: PracRepair: LLM-Empowered Automated Program Repair Inspired by Human-Like Debugging Practices
summary: As software systems grow in scale and complexity, debugging and repair remain costly and time-consuming. Large language models (LLMs) have advanced automated program repair (APR), but existing LLM-based APR approaches still largely rely on static or retrieved context, error messages, and coarse-grained validation outcomes. As a result, they underutilize dynamic information for failure understanding and repair, including failure-execution dynamics and patch-validation dynamics. Effectively leveraging such information, however, is challenging: failure-execution traces are large and noisy, raw static-dynamic context is not self-explanatory, and patch-validation dynamics are often reduced to coarse feedback. To address these challenges, we propose \textsc{PracRepair}, a fully automated LLM-based APR framework inspired by human-like debugging practices. \textsc{PracRepair} constructs an on-demand static-dynamic context from buggy programs and failure executions, performs question-driven failure diagnosis to formulate explicit repair hypotheses, and iteratively refines candidate patches using validation diagnostics and trace-level behavioral changes. Experimental results on Defects4J V1.2 and V2.0 show that \textsc{PracRepair} consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Specifically, under GPT-3.5, \textsc{PracRepair} correctly fixes 139/136 bugs on Defects4J V1.2/V2.0, while under GPT-4o it further improves to 162/171. Moreover, \textsc{PracRepair} generalizes effectively to RWB (Real-World Bugs), achieving the best performance across multiple foundation models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces \textsc{PracRepair}, an automated program repair (APR) framework utilizing large language models (LLMs) to leverage dynamic and static debugging contexts effectively, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based APR approaches fail to fully utilize dynamic failure information in debugging practices due to challenges such as noisy execution traces and coarse validation feedback.
AI.method: The proposed \textsc{PracRepair} framework employs on-demand static-dynamic context generation, question-driven failure diagnosis for repair hypotheses, and iterative patch refinement using detailed validation and behavioral diagnostics.
AI.result: \textsc{PracRepair} surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, fixing 139/136 bugs under GPT-3.5 and further improving to 162/171 under GPT-4o on Defects4J datasets, while also achieving top performance on real-world bugs.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging dynamic debugging contexts with LLMs through a structured iterative approach significantly enhances automated program repair, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness across different datasets.

title: Contactless Respiratory Monitoring on Heterogeneous Mobile Robots: A Multimodal Edge-Computing Framework
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures. To appear in Proceedings of the 8th International Workshop on IoT Applications and Industry 5.0 (IoTI5 2026), co-located with IEEE DCOSS-IoT 2026, Reykjavik, Iceland, June 2026
summary: Respiratory-rate (RR) monitoring is a critical component of remote triage and victim assessment in emergency response, disaster recovery, and infectious-disease scenarios, where minimizing physical contact can reduce responder risk and improve operational safety. However, field deployment of contactless RR monitoring remains challenging due to variable illumination, posture changes, platform heterogeneity, and the impracticality of wearable sensors in hazardous environments. In this paper, we present a modality-adaptive contactless RR monitoring framework for heterogeneous mobile robots with onboard edge computing. The proposed system combines brightness-adaptive sensor selection across RGB, thermal, near-infrared (NIR), and low-light cameras, keypoint-guided chest ROI extraction for posture-robust monitoring, and a signal-quality-index (SQI)-based filtering mechanism for reliable respiratory estimation. We implement and evaluate the framework on three robotic platforms spanning quadruped and wheeled locomotion and multiple edge-computing architectures. Experiments conducted across diverse lighting conditions, subject poses, and robot-to-subject distances demonstrate that the framework generalizes across platforms without per-platform algorithmic retuning, while revealing modality-specific operational boundaries. RGB provides the broadest coverage up to 8m, NIR remains effective up to 6m, thermal is reliable only at short range, and low-light sensing supports monitoring in complete darkness up to 8m. Overall, the results demonstrate the feasibility of multimodal contactless RR monitoring on mobile robots and support its use as a foundation for autonomous triage and victim assessment in hazardous search-and-rescue settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a system for contactless respiratory-rate monitoring using multimodal sensors on mobile robots, addressing challenges in hazardous environments with varied conditions.
AI.motivation: To enhance remote patient assessment and responder safety in hazardous scenarios like emergencies and infectious outbreaks by avoiding physical contact.
AI.method: A framework leveraging multimodal sensors (RGB, thermal, NIR, low-light), posture-robust chest ROI extraction, and signal-quality filtering, implemented across diverse robotic platforms with edge computing.
AI.result: The system was tested on various robots in diverse conditions, showing reliable and generalized performance, with each sensor modality having distinct operational ranges.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal contactless RR monitoring on mobile robots is feasible and effective for triage and victim assessment in challenging environments, with further potential for autonomous response applications.

title: SierpinskiCam: Camera-Controlled Video Retaking with Sierpinski Triangle Pattern Cues
summary: Generating novel renderings of a scene along user-defined camera trajectories from a single monocular video, dubbed video retaking, is a compelling but difficult problem in content creation and visual effects. Existing geometry-guided approaches reconstruct a 4D representation from the source video and render it along the target trajectory to condition video diffusion models. However, this guidance degrades as the target camera departs from the source trajectory, leaving newly revealed regions sparse or entirely missing. We propose SierpinskiCam, which addresses this limitation by augmenting geometry-based guidance with Sierpinski dome texture cues that contains rich trackable features even under large viewpoint changes. We further introduce a reference video conditioning mechanism that appends source-video tokens to the target-token sequence and separates the two streams with negative RoPE indices, enabling appearance grounding without architectural modification or per-video adaptation. Extensive experiments show that SierpinskiCam achieves significant gains in camera controllability, geometric consistency, and video quality across diverse and challenging retaking scenarios. Project page: https://hyelinnam.github.io/SierpinskiCam/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SierpinskiCam, a method enhancing video retaking (novel renderings from monocular videos) with improved camera controllability, geometry, and quality.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations in existing video retaking methods, where geometry-guided approaches fail for large viewpoint changes, causing missing or sparse regions.
AI.method: The approach incorporates geometry-based guidance enriched by Sierpinski dome texture cues and a reference video conditioning mechanism using source-video tokens for appearance grounding.
AI.result: Experiments validate significant improvements in geometric consistency, camera control, and video quality in various challenging scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SierpinskiCam successfully addresses limitations in video retaking by providing enhanced controllability and visual consistency, useful for content creation and visual effects.

title: Treatment Response Optimized Clinical Decision Support AI System via Digital Twin Simulation
comment: Accepted for presentation at the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Conference (EMBC) 2026
summary: Clinical decision support AI systems (CDSASs) must adapt to evolving patient conditions in real-time while adhering to strict safety constraints. We present an online adaptive framework that integrates Treatment Effect (TE) estimation to quantify clinical benefits, a patient Digital Twin (DT) to simulate treatment trajectories, and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for sequential decision-making. The AI system is initially trained on historical medical records and operates in a continuous learning loop. To ensure safety, a rule-based module monitors vital signs and blocks contraindicated treatments. Cases with strong internal model disagreement are flagged for clinician review, simulated in our experiments via a pre-trained outcome model. We validate our framework using both a synthetic clinical simulator and a real-world ovarian cancer dataset from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA). In both simulated and clinical settings, our method demonstrated superior effectiveness and stability in recommending treatments compared to standard computational baselines. Furthermore, the AI system maintains low latency and requires expert consultation for only a minority of cases in our experimental validation, demonstrating its potential as a safe, clinician-supervised tool for personalized medicine that continuously improves through practical use.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an AI-based clinical decision support system that combines Treatment Effect estimation, a patient Digital Twin, and Reinforcement Learning for real-time, personalized medicine. It is validated on synthetic and real-world datasets and ensures safety through expert review mechanisms.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a clinical AI system capable of adapting to dynamic patient conditions in real-time while maintaining strict safety standards.
AI.method: The method integrates Treatment Effect estimation for clinical benefit quantification, a patient Digital Twin for treatment simulation, and Reinforcement Learning for decision-making. A rule-based module ensures safety by blocking unsafe treatments, and strong internal disagreements are flagged for clinician review.
AI.result: The proposed framework outperforms standard computational tools in effectiveness and stability for treatment recommendations. It maintains low latency and minimizes the need for expert review, as validated on synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The AI system offers a safe and effective approach for real-time personalized medicine, combining advanced computational methods with clinician supervision to continuously improve over time.

title: Finsler Geometry, Graph Neural Networks, and You
summary: Graph neural network architectures based on the graph Laplacian approximate the Laplace-Beltrami operator, thus limiting their application to isotropic operators. As a nonlinear alternative to the Laplace-Beltrami operator, we consider estimates of the Finsler Laplacian on point clouds sampled from a manifold. We prove that these discrete estimates converge to the true operator on the manifold as the number of point samples grows. Moreover, we show that this operator can be expressed as a graph neural network layer, which we use to define a family of Finslerian graph neural networks constrained to express Finsler geometry. We show that Finslerian graph neural networks recover the geometry underlying nonlinear diffusion equations in practice.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Finslerian graph neural networks (GNNs), which use a nonlinear Finsler Laplacian operator to improve upon traditional isotropic GNNs.
AI.motivation: Graph neural networks based on the graph Laplacian are limited to isotropic operators, restricting their ability to address more general structures tied to Finsler geometry.
AI.method: The authors estimate the Finsler Laplacian on point clouds from a manifold and prove the convergence of these estimates to the true operator. These estimates are then implemented as a GNN layer to form Finslerian GNNs.
AI.result: They demonstrate that Finslerian GNNs can recover the geometry underlying nonlinear diffusion equations in practice, suggesting broader applicability.
AI.conclusion: The study positions Finslerian GNNs as a more general and effective alternative to traditional Laplacian-based GNNs for capturing complex geometries.

title: LUMEN: Coordinated Failure Recovery for Distributed LLM Serving
summary: Modern large language model (LLM) serving clusters distribute inference requests across multiple worker processes on different GPUs, but failures are prevalent at scale. When a worker fails, the cluster simultaneously loses the failed worker's GPU-resident key-value (KV) caches and serving capacity, leaving surviving workers to absorb the redirected traffic while re-running interrupted requests from scratch. Existing fault-tolerant systems either restart interrupted requests from scratch or restore KV caches from checkpoints stored on a fixed neighboring worker, but both approaches route recovery work without considering current cluster load and leave the recovering worker idle during model reload. We present LUMEN, a fault-tolerant LLM serving system that treats recovery as a load-aware coordination problem across three decision points: checkpoint placement before failures, interrupted-request distribution at failure time, and serving capacity restoration during model reload. We evaluate LUMEN using both prototype experiments and large-scale simulations and demonstrate significant improvements in serving and recovery times.

AI.tldr: LUMEN is a system for fault-tolerant large language model (LLM) serving, optimizing recovery after worker failures by coordinating checkpoint placement, request handling, and resource use.
AI.motivation: Existing fault-tolerant systems for LLM servers are inefficient when workers fail, as they either restart interrupted requests or rely on fixed placement for recovery caching, ignoring current cluster load.
AI.method: LUMEN introduces a load-aware approach to fault tolerance, optimizing three key points: checkpoint placement before failures, request distribution during failures, and serving capacity restoration during model reload.
AI.result: Experiments and simulations show LUMEN significantly improves both serving times and recovery times after worker failures.
AI.conclusion: LUMEN provides a robust and efficient solution for fault-tolerant LLM serving, addressing inefficiencies in current approaches and optimizing recovery through load-aware coordination.

title: Do Large Language Models Always Tell The Same Stories?
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled the generation of high-quality prose, yet the question of whether these models are capable of generating diverse outputs remains contested. In this work, we investigate the diversity of LLM-generated stories through the framework of narrative similarity. Using a contrastive framework and a dataset of human-written stories and prompts from r/WritingPrompts, we collect narrative similarity judgments across 10 representative LLMs, utilizing both human evaluations and three different automatic annotation methods. Our findings reveal a consistent trend: LLM-generated narratives are consistently more similar to each other than human-written stories are. We demonstrate that frontier models in particular converge on a ``mean'' generic narrative that approximates individual human stories but lacks the collective diversity of human authors. Finally, we show that common mitigation strategies, including negative prompting and temperature scaling, fail to meaningfully address this homogeneity.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the diversity of stories generated by Large Language Models (LLMs) and finds that these narratives exhibit less diversity compared to human-written stories.
AI.motivation: To assess whether LLMs can generate diverse story narratives and to explore the extent to which their outputs differ from human-authored content.
AI.method: A dataset of human-written stories from r/WritingPrompts was used to evaluate the narrative similarity of 10 LLMs. Human evaluations and three automatic annotation methods were employed under a contrastive framework.
AI.result: LLMs produce narratives that are similar to each other but less diverse compared to human-generated stories. Frontier models often create generic narratives that approximate individual human stories but lack collective diversity.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit narrative homogeneity and common strategies like negative prompting and temperature scaling are ineffective in mitigating this lack of diversity.

title: FacProcessTwin: An LLM-Based System for Process Twin Development
summary: Process twins provide real-time representations of entire production processes. By capturing how process steps interact, rather than monitoring a single machine in isolation as an asset-based digital twin does, they have the potential to drive efficiency gains across the whole process. However, developing a process twin is costly. It requires accurately modelling the entire production process: its process steps, the equipment and product-specific settings each step uses, and its process variations. The resulting model must then be bound to live operational data. We present FacProcessTwin, a system that leverages a large language model (LLM) to reduce this development time, building a process twin from a plant's process documentation and natural-language input from an operator. FacProcessTwin generates this complete process model and then automatically binds its process steps to live operational data. The generated model and its data bindings are rendered as an interactive process diagram through which manufacturing personnel can monitor and correct the system's autonomous decisions, such as resolving uncertainty at safety-critical binding steps. We evaluate FacProcessTwin through a real-world case study of an Australian food manufacturer, covering 16 production process flows that span chilled, frozen, and aseptic shelf-stable product categories and include process variations within the same product. The results show that FacProcessTwin generates these process models accurately (a mean F1 of 95.2% against ground truth) and builds each twin in roughly a sixth of the manual time. Its human-in-the-loop governance then keeps the safety-critical bindings correct: at ambiguous tags where a single-pass baseline silently mis-binds 75.0% of the time, FacProcessTwin defers to the operator and mis-binds none.

AI.tldr: FacProcessTwin leverages a large language model to efficiently develop process twins from plant documentation and operator inputs, significantly reducing development time while ensuring accurate and safe process modeling.
AI.motivation: The need to improve efficiency across entire production processes, while addressing the high cost and complexity of developing process twins, particularly in binding process models to live operational data.
AI.method: FacProcessTwin uses a large language model to generate process models from natural-language inputs and plant process documentation. It automatically binds these models to live operational data and includes human-in-the-loop governance for safety-critical corrections.
AI.result: The system accurately generated process models with a mean F1 score of 95.2%, built twins six times faster than manual methods, and avoided unsafe mis-bindings through operator intervention, achieving zero mis-bindings in critical cases.
AI.conclusion: FacProcessTwin offers a fast, accurate, and safer approach to creating process twins, significantly reducing development time and enabling human oversight for critical tasks, while demonstrating its effectiveness in a real-world manufacturing case study.

title: EgoInfinity: A Web-Scale 4D Hand-Object Interaction Data Engine for Any-View Robot Retargeting and Video-to-Action Robot Learning
summary: Internet videos constitute the largest reservoir of embodied human manipulation knowledge, yet converting arbitrary RGB footage into actionable robot training data remains a major bottleneck. Existing lab- or factory-collected datasets are narrow in scale and diversity, limiting open-world robot learning. Instead of proposing a static dataset, we introduce EgoInfinity, a universal 4D hand-object interaction data engine that enables web-scale data generation for robot retargeting and learning. EgoInfinity is a modular engine integrating perception, segmentation, reconstruction, interaction-aware refinement, and retargeting to automate this traditionally unscalable video-to-action problem without human-in-the-loop annotation. Its modular design lets the engine continuously benefit from advances in any incorporated component. With EgoInfinity, in-the-wild human manipulation videos are lifted into agent-agnostic, metric 4D hand-object representations, including hand trajectories, 6-DoF object poses, and contact-relevant states. Rather than naively connecting standalone components, EgoInfinity combines cross-module metric calibration with interaction-aware refinement to improve physical reliability, reducing drift and contact inconsistencies common in pure visual reconstruction. We further propose a novel motion retargeter that compiles the recovered 3D hand motions into executable joint trajectories for diverse robot morphologies, enabling video-to-action retargeting on any robot from arbitrary viewpoints and shot sizes (e.g., the human body is only partially visible). We validate EgoInfinity across perception fidelity, kinematic feasibility, contact consistency, cross-embodiment generalization, and real-robot skill acquisition (e.g., grasping, cutting, wiping, and pouring), demonstrating a scalable bridge from internet videos to executable robot behavior for open-world robot learning.

AI.tldr: EgoInfinity is a modular data engine designed to convert web-scale human manipulation videos into actionable training data for robots, overcoming limitations of previous datasets.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of converting diverse RGB internet videos into usable robot training data, which traditional datasets cannot adequately achieve due to their narrow diversity.
AI.method: It introduces EgoInfinity, a modular system integrating elements like perception, segmentation, 4D reconstruction, and motion retargeting to process human manipulation videos into metric-based representations for robotics.
AI.result: EgoInfinity successfully generates 4D hand-object representations and supports robot motion retargeting, validated through robot skill acquisition and improved fidelity in various metrics.
AI.conclusion: EgoInfinity provides a scalable solution for leveraging internet videos, serving as a universal bridge for robot learning in open-world scenarios.

title: FATE: Pillar Encoding and Frequency-Aware Training for Event-Based Object Detection
summary: Event cameras are bio-inspired sensors that asynchronously capture logarithmic intensity changes, offering inherent advantages in high-speed and high-dynamic-range scenarios. However, the sparse and asynchronous nature of event streams poses a fundamental challenge for modern deep learning architectures. To enable compatibility with standard models, most existing approaches partition the accumulation window into fixed temporal sub-bins. While effective for spatial processing, this internal discretization discards fine-grained temporal structure and constrains inference to the low temporal frequencies imposed by training supervision. To address this limitation, we propose FATE, a unified framework built upon a novel Pillar Encoding (PE). While operating over discrete macro-accumulation windows dictated by the target frequency, PE avoids internal temporal sub-binning. It organizes events into spatial pillars and approximates their intra-window evolution via projection onto a continuous-time orthogonal polynomial basis. This formulation yields an L2-optimal representation that retains rich temporal dynamics in a dense pseudo-image, mitigating information loss under sparse event conditions. To fully leverage this representation, we introduce Frequency-Aware Training (FAT), a soft mean-teacher curriculum that generates temporally dense pseudo-labels, effectively bridging the mismatch between low-frequency supervision and high-frequency inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FATE generalizes across architectural paradigms and consistently outperforms strong baselines. It enables robust object detection at high temporal resolutions up to 200 Hz, while incurring minimal overhead in parameter count and inference latency

AI.tldr: Event cameras produce sparse, asynchronous data which challenge typical deep learning models. FATE uses a novel Pillar Encoding (PE) and Frequency-Aware Training (FAT) to retain temporal structure and achieve high-resolution inferences at up to 200 Hz.
AI.motivation: Event cameras have unique advantages for high-speed, high-dynamic-range scenarios but their asynchronous outputs make integration with standard deep learning methods difficult. Current methods lose temporal details and limit performance due to low-frequency supervision.
AI.method: The paper presents FATE, which introduces a Pillar Encoding (PE) to organize event data into spatial pillars while approximating temporal evolution with continuous-time orthogonal polynomial basis. Additionally, it proposes Frequency-Aware Training (FAT) to enhance training through dense pseudo-labels, allowing high-frequency inference.
AI.result: FATE demonstrates strong generalization and outperforms baselines, achieving robust object detection at high temporal resolutions (up to 200 Hz) with minimal extra computation and latency.
AI.conclusion: FATE effectively addresses the challenge of processing sparse, asynchronous event camera data. By preserving temporal details and aligning training supervision with high-frequency inferences, it enhances performance across architectures with minimal trade-offs.

title: Incumbent Advantage: Brand Bias and Cognitive Manipulation Dynamics in LLM Recommendation Systems
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are becoming a major way for consumers to find products, but we do not yet understand how brands compete in this new channel. We study brand dynamics in LLM recommendations using skincare products -- a category where consumers cannot easily judge quality before buying and must rely on brand reputation -- across three commercial LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Sonnet, Gemini 3 Flash), with a robustness check on search goods. In three experiments, we find: (1) a Conditional Monopoly where well-known brands get recommended 100% of the time (IAI = 10.0) when all products have the same specifications, but this dominance disappears with less than a +0.1-star rating advantage for a competitor; (2) authority-style marketing language, including fabricated clinical-evidence claims, breaks this monopoly at a Bias Surplus Value equal to +0.17 rating points, with each model responding differently; and (3) a social dilemma in multi-brand GEO competition: when all brands adopt the same optimization strategy, individual payoff falls from +0.802 to +0.007 in our payoff proxy, and non-participating brands receive zero recommendations in our tests.
