A lightweight Schema Selector first prunes the full database schema to the relevant tables and columns. An LLM Judger then decides whether the question requires decomposition: straightforward questions follow a direct generation path and complex ones are escalated to a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of atomic sub-questions, each solved by a targeted SQL generation step. A RAG component grounds the decomposer with semantically similar training examples, and a Topology Refiner restructures the reasoning plan when execution failures signal a flawed decomposition rather than a fixable SQL error. DecoSearch achieves 70.53% execution accuracy on BIRD and 88.31% on Spider with a DeepSeek backbone, surpassing all training-free baselines while consuming an order of magnitude fewer tokens than competing methods. It also functions as a model-agnostic wrapper, consistently improving fine-tuned SQL generation backbones without any modification to the pipeline.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DecoSearch, a framework to improve SQL generation from natural language queries by routing them through dynamic reasoning pathways.
AI.motivation: The paper tackles the challenge of improving SQL generation on complex queries that require multi-step and data-aware reasoning, as existing methods perform poorly in such cases.
AI.method: DecoSearch is a training-free framework that uses a Schema Selector to prune irrelevant database elements, an LLM Judger to decide if questions need decomposition, and a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for breaking down complex queries into atomic sub-questions. It employs a RAG component for grounding and a Topology Refiner for plan adjustment in case of execution failures.
AI.result: DecoSearch achieves 70.53% execution accuracy on BIRD and 88.31% on Spider datasets using a DeepSeek backbone, outperforming other training-free baselines and using significantly fewer tokens.
AI.conclusion: DecoSearch effectively enhances SQL generation accuracy for complex queries while requiring fewer resources, and it serves as a model-agnostic wrapper that can improve other fine-tuned SQL generation systems without altering their pipelines.

title: Credibility-Weighted Pricing of Autonomous Vehicle Liability Under Operational Design Domain Shift
summary: Automated Driving System deployments create a foundational ratemaking challenge: sparse experience, shifting operational design domains, and non-stationary risk across software releases. We propose a hierarchical Bayesian credibility framework pooling across cities, software versions, and territories via a learned ODD-similarity kernel, nesting Buhlmann-Straub as a limiting case. Demonstrated on 648 verified-engaged Waymo crashes across four U.S. metros from the NHTSA Standing General Order database against 116 million matched miles, city-aggregate credibility weights are moderate (0.12-0.46), partial pooling decisively outperforms no pooling, and a power analysis shows the learned kernel's advantage becomes detectable at approximately twelve deployed cities.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses challenges in automated driving system ratemaking due to sparse and shifting data. It proposes a hierarchical Bayesian credibility framework and demonstrates its potential with real-world crash and mileage data, showing partial pooling outperforms other methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address foundational challenges in ratemaking for automated driving systems, arising from sparse data, evolving operational design domains, and non-stationary risks across software updates.
AI.method: The authors propose a hierarchical Bayesian credibility framework that leverages pooling across various dimensions like cities, software versions, and territories using a learned similarity kernel. The Buhlmann-Straub model is nested within as a special case.
AI.result: Applied to real-world crash data from Waymo across four U.S. cities totaling 116 million miles, the framework found that city-level aggregate credibility weights ranged between 0.12-0.46. Partial data pooling outperformed no pooling, and the learned kernel's impact was detectable with as few as twelve cities.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that hierarchical Bayesian approaches, particularly with partial pooling, address the unique challenges in automated driving system ratemaking and are superior to traditional methods in handling sparse and evolving datasets.

title: When Multiple Scripts Matter: Evaluating ASR in Clinical Settings
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) in non-English clinical settings is challenged by multiscript variability, where the same term may appear in multiple valid orthographic forms. Conventional string-matching evaluation metrics often underestimate ASR performance by treating orthographic variants as errors. To address this issue, we introduce MultiClin, a clinical ASR benchmark designed to evaluate robustness to multiscript variability. Experiments across diverse ASR models show that multiscript-aware evaluation provides a fairer assessment of recognition quality than conventional single-reference evaluation. We further investigate the impact of script consistency during training and find that inconsistent script mappings increase orthographic uncertainty and hinder model convergence, with a balanced 50% mapping ratio producing the highest entropy. In contrast, script unification consistently yields the best ASR performance. Our dataset and code are publicly available at: https://github.com/aitrics-ronaldo/Interspeech_MultiClin.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenge of multiscript variability in non-English clinical ASR systems by introducing a new benchmark, MultiClin, and explores the effects of script consistency during training.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve the evaluation and training of automatic speech recognition systems in non-English clinical contexts, where multiscript variability poses a challenge.
AI.method: The authors developed MultiClin, a benchmark designed to evaluate ASR robustness to multiscript variability, and conducted experiments on various ASR models while analyzing the effects of script consistency during training.
AI.result: The study found that multiscript-aware evaluations provide a fairer assessment of ASR performance compared to conventional methods. Script consistency during training was shown to influence orthographic uncertainty and model convergence, with script unification yielding the best performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating multiscript-aware evaluation and emphasizing script unification during training improves the robustness and performance of ASR systems in non-English clinical settings.

title: WAM-RL: World-Action Model Reinforcement Learning with Reconstruction Rewards and Online Video SFT
summary: Recent World-Action (WA) models demonstrate strong generalization ability and data efficiency, but they typically rely on expert trajectories for training. This reliance limits their ability to acquire fine-grained manipulation skills beyond the demonstration distribution and prevents them from continuously improving through real-world interaction. To address these limitations, we propose WAM-RL, a reinforcement learning framework that enables joint optimization of the world model and the action model through online interaction with the environment. By allowing the two components to co-evolve, our approach enhances fine-grained control and adaptability. Specifically, a WA model consists of a world model and an actor. We design a tailored reinforcement learning method with hierarchical optimization to coordinate their improvement. On the methodological side, we systematically investigate the effects of applying reinforcement learning to the action model, as well as online training of the world model within an RL setting. Our experiments reveal a key insight: optimizing only the actor yields improvements on short-horizon tasks, but fails to provide significant gains on long-horizon tasks. In contrast, jointly optimizing both the world model and the actor is critical for achieving strong performance in long-horizon settings. Our work is the first to introduce reinforcement learning into the World-Action paradigm, and provides insights into how online optimization of both the action head and the world model impacts overall performance.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes WAM-RL, a framework that enhances world-action (WA) models by integrating reinforcement learning (RL), enabling joint optimization of both the actor and the world model, improving fine-grained control and adaptability.
AI.motivation: Traditional world-action models rely heavily on expert demonstrations, limiting their ability to develop fine-grained skills outside the demonstration distribution, and hindering continuous improvement through real-world interaction.
AI.method: The authors developed a reinforcement learning framework (WAM-RL) for WA models, which allows joint evolution of an actor (action model) and a world model through online training, using a tailored RL method with hierarchical optimization.
AI.result: Experiments showed that optimizing only the action model benefits short-horizon tasks but is insufficient for long-horizon tasks. Joint optimization of both the world model and action model is critical for superior long-horizon task performance.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces reinforcement learning to enhance WA models, offering key insights into the interplay between optimizing the action and world models for better performance, especially in long-horizon tasks.

title: Contact-Based Fringe Projection Profilometry for High-Resolution 3-D Surface Measurement of Reflective and Transparent Objects
summary: This paper presents a contact-based 3-D surface measurement method based on a Digital Fringe Projection (DFP) system, belonging to the vision-based tactile sensing family pioneered by the commercially successful GelSight sensor. Such sensors have proven effective for robotic fingertip manipulation and contact sensing. However, because GelSight employs photometric stereo with RGB LEDs, it does not measure absolute depth directly but instead infers it by integrating estimated surface gradients, which can accumulate reconstruction errors; in addition, it becomes increasingly difficult to calibrate as the sensing area grows, and its depth accuracy is challenged on highly reflective or transparent objects. To overcome these drawbacks, we propose a fringe-projection-based contact measurement technique that performs triangulation-based 3-D reconstruction on a coated silicone contact surface, providing dense per-pixel surface geometry and full-field 3-D shape measurement over the contact region. By integrating high-accuracy digital fringe projection into the sensor, our approach simplifies calibration over larger areas and enhances depth precision for complex surfaces. Experimental results, including a direct comparison with a GelSight Mini sensor, a sphere-fitting accuracy evaluation, and an uncertainty analysis, confirm that the proposed method significantly improves the accuracy and stability of structured-light-based 3-D measurements, allowing reliable reconstruction of objects with diverse optical properties.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a fringe-projection-based tactile sensing method for improved 3-D surface measurement, addressing limitations of existing GelSight sensors.
AI.motivation: Current tactile sensors like GelSight face depth inaccuracy due to indirect measurement techniques and challenges in calibrating large areas or handling objects with reflective/transparent surfaces.
AI.method: A Digital Fringe Projection system is employed for triangulation-based 3-D reconstruction, offering direct depth measurement and enhanced calibration over larger areas.
AI.result: Experimental comparisons show the proposed method achieves higher accuracy, stability, and reliability for diverse optical surfaces than GelSight.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enhances the precision of tactile sensing systems and expands their applicability to complex surfaces and larger sensing areas.

title: WallZero: Mastering the Game of WallGo with Strategic Analysis
comment: Accepted by the Computers and Games conference (CG 2026)
summary: WallGo is a recently introduced strategic board game popularized by the 2025 Netflix series The Devil's Plan. Although played on a small 7 x 7 board, its combination of stone movement and wall placement yields high game-tree complexity and intricate strategic interactions. Despite its growing popularity, WallGo remains underexplored. This paper presents WallZero, an AlphaZero-based agent for the two-player WallGo setting. We introduce tailored action and feature designs to improve playing performance significantly. In the evaluation, WallZero defeats two professional Go players who participated in this study, securing on average 1.98x more territory per game. Beyond its strength, we use WallZero to assess game fairness and identify key strategies for mastering WallGo. Interestingly, our results show that the opening used in the Netflix series yields a more balanced game. Our code is available at https://rlg.iis.sinica.edu.tw/papers/wallzero.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WallZero, a strategic agent for WallGo based on AlphaZero, demonstrating its high performance and contributing insights into the game's fairness and key strategies.
AI.motivation: To understand and advance the study of WallGo, an underexplored yet complex strategic game that is gaining popularity.
AI.method: Developed WallZero using AlphaZero framework with tailored action and feature designs for WallGo, and evaluated its strength against professional players.
AI.result: WallZero outperformed professional Go players, securing on average 1.98x more territory per game. It also assisted in assessing game fairness and key strategies.
AI.conclusion: WallZero proves effective in mastering WallGo, highlighting the impact of strategic openings and contributing to understanding its dynamics.

title: Operator Boosting Produces Pareto-Efficient PDE Surrogates
comment: 19 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Preprint submitted to Elsevier
summary: Neural operators are widely used as surrogate solution maps for partial differential equations (PDEs), but full-size models can be costly to store, deploy, and evaluate in many-query scientific workflows. This work introduces Operator Boosting, a stagewise residual-learning framework for constructing compact neural-operator surrogates directly, rather than training a large model and compressing it afterward. Starting from the empirical mean predictor in normalized output coordinates, the method trains a sequence of tiny same-family neural operators on residual fields and incorporates each correction through validation-selected shrinkage. We instantiate the framework with Fourier neural operators (FNOs), DeepONets, and convolutional neural operators (CNOs), and compare boosted tiny stacks against full-size monolithic baselines across one-, two-, and three-dimensional PDE benchmarks from PDEBench, APEBench, and The Well. Across 30 dataset-architecture pairs, 21 show positive mean accuracy gains and 17 have positive confidence intervals, while all boosted stacks reduce trainable parameter count by approximately 72-95%. Best-model comparisons show empirical Pareto improvements on 7 of 10 completed PDE benchmarks, including two-dimensional Navier-Stokes, shallow-water dynamics, Darcy flow, one-dimensional transport and reaction systems, and three-dimensional compressible Navier-Stokes. These results show that Operator Boosting often improves the empirical accuracy-parameter Pareto frontier of neural PDE surrogates, while also exposing PDE- and architecture-dependent regimes where residual boosting fails to offset compression.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Operator Boosting, which constructs compact neural operators for solving PDEs without requiring post-training compression, and demonstrates improved efficiency and accuracy for many cases.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the high storage, deployment, and evaluation costs of full-size neural operators in solving PDEs, by creating smaller, efficient surrogates directly through a stagewise residual-learning process.
AI.method: The method introduces Operator Boosting, a framework where tiny neural operators are trained sequentially on residual fields, starting from an empirical mean predictor. Shrinkage factors are selected through validation, and the approach is instantiated using FNOs, DeepONets, and CNOs.
AI.result: Operator Boosting consistently reduces trainable parameters by 72-95% and delivers accuracy gains in many cases across PDE benchmarks. It achieves improvements in 21 out of 30 dataset-architecture pairs, showing Pareto improvements in 7 out of 10 completed benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Operator Boosting offers a lightweight and effective alternative for neural operators in PDE tasks, but it also identifies cases where residual boosting cannot overcome compression challenges.

title: Perceptual compensation for tonal context in self-supervised speech models
summary: This study examines the extent to which the wav2vec2.0 architecture exhibits evidence of compensation for phonological context. We conducted a pseudo-replication of a perceptional compensation experiment on Mandarin Chinese tones, and compared the embedding similarities and probing classifier outputs between a purely self-supervised pre-trained model and a model fine-tuned for Mandarin ASR. No evidence of compensation was found in the embedding similarities of the purely pre-trained model. Probing classifiers showed some evidence of compensation in addition to the expected layer-wise improvements in categorization, but failed to replicate human performance on isolated test syllables. Our findings contrast with previous reports of sensitivity to phonological structure emerging through pre-training alone, and suggest that supervised objectives may be necessary to encourage the abstraction of at least some types of phonological regularities.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how well wav2vec2.0 represents phonological context in Mandarin tones and finds that supervised training is likely needed to capture certain phonological abstractions.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand whether the wav2vec2.0 model, a self-supervised architecture, can demonstrate compensation for phonological contexts in Mandarin tones.
AI.method: Researchers conducted a pseudo-replication of a phonological perception experiment on Mandarin tones and analyzed embedding similarities and probing classifier outputs, comparing pre-trained and fine-tuned wav2vec2.0 models.
AI.result: The pre-trained model showed no evidence of phonological compensation in embedding similarities, while fine-tuned models exhibited partial compensation. However, none matched human performance on isolated syllable testing.
AI.conclusion: The study suggests that unsupervised pre-training alone might not be sufficient for certain phonological abstractions; supervised training appears necessary to capture these complexities.

title: PearlVLA: Progressive Embodied Action-Plan Refinement in Latent Space
summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models face a trade-off between efficient action generation and explicit deliberation. Directly decoding actions from vision-language backbone representations enables low-latency control, whereas explicit reasoning through textual chains, pixel-level subgoals, or action search can improve planning but incurs substantial latency and computational cost. We propose PearlVLA, a VLA framework that moves deliberation into the latent space of a vision-language model (VLM). PearlVLA separates VLM meta-query representations into a fixed visual grounding branch and an iterative latent plan branch. At each refinement round, a plan-conditioned world query probes a lightweight frozen latent world model for an action-free future observation latent, which is fed back to guide plan refinement. A future-guided RefineNet then applies scheduled residual updates to progressively refine a coarse semantic draft into a fine-grained latent action plan. The refined plan after K rounds is then decoded in parallel into an action chunk for low-latency execution. We further introduce Causal Refinement-Grouped Process-Reward RL to optimize the latent refinement process with rewards from longer-horizon imagined futures induced by latent plan edits. Empirical evaluations on the LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that PearlVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance among existing methods.

AI.tldr: PearlVLA improves vision-language-action models by integrating the deliberation process into the latent space, providing both efficient action generation and planning.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models struggle to balance quick decision-making with detailed planning, leading to either higher latency or reduced control efficiency.
AI.method: The framework separates the vision-language meta-query into visual grounding and latent planning. PearlVLA refines latent action plans progressively using a RefineNet and introduces a novel reinforcement learning method to optimize this process.
AI.result: PearlVLA achieves state-of-the-art results on the LIBERO benchmark, showing superior planning and efficiency compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: By incorporating deliberation into latent space, PearlVLA enables a more efficient and effective balance of planning and rapid action generation in VLA systems.

title: WeaveLA: Event Driven Cross-Subtask Latent Memory Weaving for Repetitive Robot Manipulation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies have achieved remarkable single-step manipulation, yet they remain brittle precisely where each stage depends on what was just completed. The core issue is structural: short-window VLAs lack an explicit channel for rouxting information across sub-task boundaries, and existing memory-augmented variants either write at every frame, retrieve from demonstration-time stages, or fire at sub-goal events without performing an explicit sub-task-to-sub-task hand-off into the action expert. We identify the sub-goal completion event as the natural temporal unit for cross-subtask memory hand-off, and present WeaveLA (Weave Latent memory for Vision-Language-Action policies), a cross-subtask memory interface that, on top of a frozen VLA backbone, compresses each completed segment into latent tokens via query-driven attention pooling and routes them directly into the action-generation path of the next sub-task. This event-triggered, action-side design preserves the base policy's short-window interface while adding a lightweight cross-subtask channel. Through stratified evaluation on RoboMME with a $π_{0.5}$ backbone, WeaveLA's gains land exactly where the channel is needed: on the hardest repetition slice (SwingXtimes, $N{=}3$), success rises from $0\%$ to $47.8\%$, while single-execution episodes remain unchanged. Per-episode paired analysis confirms the gains are confined to tasks whose causal structure requires cross-subtask information.

AI.tldr: WeaveLA (Weave Latent memory for Vision-Language-Action policies) introduces a memory interface to explicitly hand off information across sub-task boundaries, improving task completions in scenarios requiring memory.
AI.motivation: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies struggle with tasks involving interdependent sub-tasks due to a lack of explicit mechanisms for memory transfer across sub-task boundaries.
AI.method: WeaveLA incorporates an event-triggered memory interface that compresses completed sub-task segments into latent tokens and directly routes them into the action generation for subsequent sub-tasks.
AI.result: WeaveLA significantly improves performance on complex tasks requiring memory (e.g., SwingXtimes, N=3), with success rates increasing from 0% to 47.8% without impacting single-step tasks.
AI.conclusion: WeaveLA's design addresses memory bottlenecks in VLA policies, making them more reliable for handling sequences of interdependent sub-tasks.

title: A homotopy-type-theoretic generalization of neurosymbolic inference
summary: A wide range of neurosymbolic (NeSy) systems compute one functional: a belief-weighted sum of a logical quantity over a space of $σ$-structures, of which weighted model counting, fuzzy logic, and probabilistic logic are special cases. This account is built on sets, and a set deliberately forgets two things that are important for NeSy: when two $σ$-structures are the same up to a symmetry of the theory, and how many distinct proofs witness a query. Replacing the underlying sets by types, in the sense of homotopy type theory, preserves this information, and turns this functional into a belief-weighted homotopy cardinality, a notion of size that counts each object in inverse proportion to its symmetries. We develop the framework from scratch for NeSy systems, prove a conservativity theorem that recovers the classical functional when symmetries are trivial, and show that the symmetry our framework exposes is exactly the one behind reasoning shortcuts. The payoff is concrete: the shortcut-aware concept posterior that recent methods reach by ensembling or expressive density estimation is the only symmetry-invariant point of the confusion-set simplex, computable in closed form by averaging a single model over the symmetry group. On MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks this single-model wrapper is better calibrated than a diversity-trained ensemble, while leaving label accuracy and identifiable concepts untouched. Code is freely available at https://github.com/bio-ontology-research-group/hott-nesy.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a homotopy type theory framework for neurosymbolic (NeSy) systems that explicitly accounts for symmetries in reasoning and proof enumeration, offering a more efficient and calibrated approach to reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional NeSy systems overlook symmetries among structures and multiple proofs for queries, which are important for capturing reasoning shortcuts and understanding belief-weighted sums. Addressing these limitations can enhance computational and interpretive efficiency in NeSy systems.
AI.method: The authors replace sets with types from homotopy type theory to preserve symmetry information and proof multiplicity. They define belief-weighted homotopy cardinality and demonstrate its connection to symmetry-invariant reasoning. The framework recovers classical results when symmetries are trivial and extends reasoning capabilities through symmetry-aware methods.
AI.result: The proposed framework enables closed-form computation of symmetry-aware reasoning shortcuts, eliminating the need for complex ensembling. It performs better calibration on MNIST reasoning-shortcut benchmarks compared to diversity-trained ensembles, without compromising on label accuracy or concept identification.
AI.conclusion: By integrating symmetries into reasoning processes, the framework simplifies reasoning computations and enhances calibration of NeSy systems while retaining accuracy. This innovation paves the way for more efficient and interpretable NeSy applications.

title: ResAware: Cross-Environment Website Fingerprinting via Resource-Privileged Distillation
summary: While Website Fingerprinting (WF) attacks achieve high accuracy in controlled laboratory settings, they often degrade substantially in real-world environments due to spatio-temporal drift, browser heterogeneity, proxy obfuscation and etc. This limitation stems from their sole reliance on low-level traffic features that are noisy and highly sensitive to environmental perturbations. To address this problem, we propose \textbf{ResAware}, a cross-environment resource-aware distillation framework under a \textit{training-rich/inference-poor} asymmetric setting. Specifically, ResAware trains a teacher model on resource-level features, and then distills the resulting privileged knowledge into a student model through heterogeneous knowledge distillation. At deployment time, the student model performs inference using only encrypted traffic, incurring zero additional cost. We evaluate ResAware on a large-scale dataset collected over five months from six globally distributed vantage points, comprising more than $160{,}000$ paired samples. The results show that ResAware significantly enhances the cross-environment robustness of diverse WF baselines. Under a 150-day temporal drift, for example, ResAware improves the F1-score of Var-CNN from $72.77\%$ to $81.49\%$ and the open-world $TPR@1\%FPR$ from $22.40\%$ to $27.20\%$. Our results demonstrate that resource-level supervision improves WF robustness without expanding online observation capabilities.

AI.tldr: ResAware leverages resource-aware distillation to improve Website Fingerprinting (WF) attack robustness across diverse environments while using only encrypted traffic during inference.
AI.motivation: Existing WF attacks rely heavily on low-level traffic features, which are noisy and fail in real-world scenarios due to environmental drift and heterogeneity.
AI.method: The paper proposes the ResAware framework, which distills resource-level knowledge from a teacher model to a student model using heterogeneous knowledge distillation, allowing inference solely on encrypted traffic with no added cost.
AI.result: ResAware enhances WF robustness, improving metrics like F1-score from 72.77% to 81.49% under a 150-day temporal drift and TPR@1%FPR from 22.40% to 27.20%.
AI.conclusion: The study proves that resource-level supervision robustly improves WF performance while maintaining cost-effectiveness and cross-environment adaptability.

title: Environment-Grounded Automated Prompt Optimization for LLM Game Agents
summary: LLM agents in interactive environments are highly sensitive to their prompts, yet prompt engineering remains a manual, task-specific process. We introduce an automated prompt optimization framework for LLM agents that decomposes the observation-to-action pipeline into a goal-conditioned descriptor agent and an action selection agent, and iteratively refines each module's prompt through an LLM-driven evolutionary loop guided by environment returns. We propose a behavior analyzer to attribute episode outcomes to specific prompt components, and a mutator to propose targeted revisions to the prompt, before validating them through environment rollouts. We evaluate on all five BabyAI tasks in the BALROG benchmark, comparing our pipeline against BALROG's RobustCoTAgent under both plain and guided prompt initializations. Optimization improves performance consistently across tasks and conditions, without requiring updates to the model weights. On PutNext, a multi-step coordination task where the RobustCoTAgent achieves 0% success, our framework reaches up to 72.5% success rate using the same underlying LLM with optimized prompts. These results suggest that a multi-agent framework, combined with automatic prompt optimization, enhances LLMs without the need for fine-tuning or extensive human supervision.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an automated framework to optimize prompts for LLM agents, increasing their task performance in interactive environments without changing model weights or requiring human fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Manually engineering prompts for LLM agents in interactive environments is task-specific and labor-intensive, yet prompts significantly affect performance. Automating this process could improve efficiency and outcomes.
AI.method: The authors propose a two-module framework with a descriptor agent and an action selection agent. Using an LLM-driven evolutionary loop, alongside behavior analysis and mutation-based revision, prompts are iteratively optimized for environment-specific tasks.
AI.result: The approach significantly improves performance across five BabyAI benchmark tasks. In the PutNext task, the framework recorded a 72.5% success rate compared to 0% of the baseline agent using the same LLM.
AI.conclusion: Automatic prompt optimization, integrated with multi-agent frameworks, can consistently enhance the performance of LLMs on interactive tasks without additional model fine-tuning or excessive human intervention.

title: SPARK: Low Latency Single-Camera 3D Pose Estimation for Autonomous Racing using Keypoints
summary: In autonomous racing, fast detection of other participants' movements is required to plan safe, collision-free trajectories with non-cooperative opponents. LiDAR detection is inherently slower and harder to deploy on edge devices than vision methods, causing delayed detections that limit object tracking performance during high-dynamic maneuvering. Utilizing monocular 3D detection enables an easy-to-deploy, low-latency detection of other participants on the racetrack. We present SPARK, a single-camera pose-estimation algorithm for autonomous racing using keypoint detection. It achieves long-range detection with high accuracy, exceeding the performance of state-of-the-art monocular camera detection algorithms while maintaining lower latency. By employing well-optimized YOLO models and leveraging the fixed geometry in the autonomous racing domain, the algorithm also exhibits low latency and resource usage. We evaluate the performance of our approach on real-world autonomous racing data and compare it to state-of-the-art LiDAR and camera detection algorithms. The source code is available at: https://github.com/TUMFTM/SPARK-camera-det

AI.tldr: SPARK is a fast pose-estimation algorithm using a single camera optimized for autonomous racing, achieving highly accurate and low-latency detection compared to competing methods.
AI.motivation: The need for fast and efficient participant movement detection in autonomous racing demands improved methods over LiDAR due to its higher latency and resource constraints.
AI.method: SPARK utilizes keypoint detection with optimized YOLO models and exploits the fixed geometry of the autonomous racing domain for monocular 3D detection.
AI.result: SPARK demonstrated high accuracy in long-range detections and reduced latency compared to state-of-the-art detection methods, accommodating real-world autonomous racing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SPARK offers an advanced solution for low-latency, high-accuracy pose detection suitable for autonomous racing, overcoming limitations of existing LiDAR and monocular camera detection systems.

title: StereoFactory: A Unified Merging Framework for Robust Stereo Matching
summary: Stereo matching has advanced through foundation models trained on large-scale datasets, yet this paradigm suffers from a scalability bottleneck: incorporating new data requires costly joint retraining. Model merging offers a scalable post-hoc alternative by integrating knowledge from specialized models after source checkpoints are available. However, existing merging methods typically retain all available models or rely on greedy inclusion, which can preserve harmful task-vector interference. We propose StereoFactory, a coarse-to-fine evolutionary framework for adaptive model merging. Stage~1 employs a genetic algorithm to search the combinatorial space of model subsets, determining which models should participate. Stage~2 addresses module-level knowledge specialization (different functional modules exhibit distinct preferences for knowledge sources) through CMA-ES optimization of architecture-adaptive routing over the selected task vectors, with optional module-level scaling. Experiments across two architectures and four benchmarks demonstrate that StereoFactory consistently achieves the best four-benchmark average under the same checkpoint pool, reducing the average error from 3.80 to 3.30 on NMRF and from 2.88 to 2.19 on FoundationStereo relative to the strongest controlled baseline. The post-hoc search requires only 2.7--3.7\% of the corresponding joint-retraining wall-clock time. Analysis reveals that knowledge contributions are inherently module-specific, and selected subsets can transfer across architectures with minimal degradation. Code will be publicly released upon acceptance at: https://github.com/XiandaGuo/StereoFactory.

AI.tldr: StereoFactory introduces a method for merging knowledge from specialized stereo matching models without costly joint retraining, significantly reducing errors and computational time.
AI.motivation: Scaling stereo matching models by incorporating new data is impractical due to the resource-intensive nature of joint retraining, necessitating efficient post-hoc alternatives.
AI.method: StereoFactory employs a two-stage process: genetic algorithms identify optimal model subsets, and CMA-ES optimizes architecture-specific routing and scaling of task vectors.
AI.result: StereoFactory reduces errors on benchmarks outperforming baselines, achieving reduced error rates of 3.30 and 2.19 for NMRF and FoundationStereo, respectively, with less computational time.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively merges specialized models, avoids task-vector interference, and generalizes across architectures while being computationally efficient.

title: FlowRAG: Synergizing Explicit Reasoning via Frequency-Aware Multi-Granularity Graph Flow
summary: Graph-based retrieval-augmented generation (GraphRAG) is effective for knowledge-intensive and multi-hop query tasks; however, many existing methods primarily seed entity-based graphs and rely on implicit semantic relevance propagation. This often (i) under-retrieves when user queries are abstract and semantically sparse at the entity level, and (ii) suffers from brittle multi-hop reasoning, where noisy activations can derail entity-to-entity transitions and corrupt the inferred relation chain, yielding unreliable conclusions. To this end, we propose \texttt{FlowRAG}, a semantic-aware retrieval framework that improves both semantic recall and explicit reasoning. Specifically, \texttt{FlowRAG} constructs a quad-level heterogeneous graph over passages, summaries, sentences, and entities, where summary nodes serve as a coarse semantic hub. At retrieval time, a dual-granularity activation module combines summary--query alignment with sentence-level matching to activate relevant entities under paraphrase and abstraction robustly. We then introduce a frequency-aware weighted flow module that routes relevance through entity--passage links weighted by within-passage term frequency, pruning noisy connections and extracting high-confidence reasoning paths as an explicit logic skeleton for generation. Extensive experiments show that \texttt{FlowRAG} obtains state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FlowRAG, a novel semantic-aware retrieval framework that enhances semantic recall and explicit reasoning in knowledge-intensive tasks by using a heterogeneous graph structure and advanced modules for robust retrieval and reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based retrieval systems often underperform with abstract queries and struggle with reliable multi-hop reasoning due to noisy activations and fragile entity transitions.
AI.method: FlowRAG builds a quad-level heterogeneous graph including passages, summaries, sentences, and entities, and introduces a dual-granularity activation module for better query alignment. It also proposes a frequency-aware weighted flow module to prune noisy data and improve reasoning accuracy.
AI.result: FlowRAG achieves state-of-the-art results in benchmarks focused on complex reasoning tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness in semantic retrieval and reliable logical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: FlowRAG significantly improves reasoning reliability and semantic recall, making it a standout framework for complex, multi-hop, knowledge-intensive query tasks.

title: CheckMIABench: Firm Foundations For Membership Inference Attacks on Language Models
summary: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are a canonical way to assess a machine learning model's privacy properties. Although several attempts have been made to evaluate MIAs on language models, the extant literature has suffered numerous difficulties in constructing clean evaluations to test new techniques. In particular, subtle distribution shifts between member and non-member sets can undermine the statistical validity of MIAs; recent work has underscored this by showing that "blind" methods with no access to the underlying model can perform far better than published methods on the same benchmarks. This paper constructs a benchmark for principled evaluation of MIAs against LLMs, by leveraging the insight that training data before and after a fixed point during training are drawn from the same distribution. Therefore, all open-source models with intermediate checkpoints and public training data can be converted into MIA testbeds. We apply our framework to a half-dozen published attacks on the Pythia and OLMo family of models, from 70M to 7B parameters. To facilitate further privacy research, we open-source a modular library for designing and implementing attacks in this setting: https://github.com/safr-ai-lab/pandora_llm.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a novel and robust benchmark for evaluating membership inference attacks (MIAs) against language models (LLMs) by leveraging open-source models and their intermediate checkpoints.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address existing challenges and biases in evaluating MIAs, particularly those stemming from distribution shifts between member and non-member data, and to enable statistically valid benchmarks for LLM privacy research.
AI.method: The authors utilize the same-distribution property of training data before and after a specific training point to create principled MIA benchmarks. They test their framework on various published attacks using open-source LLMs and provide a public library for such research.
AI.result: The framework was successfully applied to several attacks on Pythia and OLMo models across a range of parameters (70M to 7B), demonstrating its effectiveness. Additionally, they released an open-source library to support future privacy research.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a robust benchmark for evaluating MIAs against LLMs, addressing previous limitations in statistical validity. By making tools open-source, it encourages further exploration into machine learning privacy.

title: GameCraft-Bench: Can Agents Build Playable Games End-to-End in a Real Game Engine?
summary: Game generation is an emerging application of coding agents, requiring models to transform natural-language specifications into playable interactive systems. Unlike traditional coding tasks, game generation takes place within a game engine, where scripts, scenes, assets, rendering, and runtime interactions must jointly produce coherent gameplay. We formalize end-to-end game generation as the problem of producing a complete game artifact that realizes a specification through observable player-game interaction in a target environment. We argue that evaluating this setting requires three desiderata: Engine Grounding, Artifact Completeness, and Interactive Verification. We propose an interaction-grounded evaluation framework that assesses executable gameplay through replayed demonstrations and rubric-guided multimodal judging. We instantiate this framework as GameCraft-Bench, a benchmark comprising 140 Godot tasks across 15 game families. Evaluations of frontier coding agents show that end-to-end game generation remains highly challenging: the strongest agent achieves only 41.46%, and most agents score below 40%. Further analysis reveals that while agents often implement recognizable mechanics, they struggle to deliver complete games with sufficient content, functional visual feedback, and coherent presentation. See https://tongxuluo.github.io/gamecraft-bench-website for demos, code, and data.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GameCraft-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating the ability of coding agents to generate end-to-end games based on natural-language specifications. Current coding agents still perform poorly on this complex task.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of transforming natural-language game specifications into fully playable interactive systems, recognizing the unique complexity of this task compared to traditional coding.
AI.method: They propose an interaction-grounded evaluation framework focusing on Engine Grounding, Artifact Completeness, and Interactive Verification, and they develop GameCraft-Bench with 140 tasks in the Godot engine to test this.
AI.result: Evaluations show that end-to-end game generation is currently very challenging, with the best agent achieving only 41.46% and others scoring even lower. Agents often fail to create complete and coherent games.
AI.conclusion: Game generation is an emerging but demanding task, and current coding models require significant advancement to meet the requirements for producing coherent and complete games.

title: ThinkingVLA: Interleaved Vision and Language Reasoning for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Most Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models map observations directly to actions without explicit reasoning, limiting their capacity for reasoning-intensive long-horizon tasks. To address this, existing approaches adopt Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to enable subgoal decomposition and spatial anticipation. However, those methods lack a unified architecture for effective cross-modal reasoning and fail to explicitly include inverse reasoning ability based on the target state. We argue that manipulation planning naturally decomposes into prediction, anticipating the next visual state, and inverse dynamics, inferring the actions to reach it. Bridging both requires a unified autoregressive architecture that interleaves textual and visual reasoning in a single generation process. We propose \textbf{ThinkingVLA}, a generative model that realizes this decomposition within a unified Mixture-of-Transformers architecture. ThinkingVLA consists of a forward CoT that identifies the immediate subgoal and guides the visual forecasting; the predicted image then serves as the target state, grounding an inverse CoT that reasons about spatial relationships and action intent based on the predicted image; and the final action is generated conditioned on this full reasoning context. Extensive experiments on simulation and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that ThinkingVLA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with particularly large gains on long-horizon manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ThinkingVLA, a generative Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model with a novel method for reasoning-intensive long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action models struggle with reasoning and long-horizon tasks due to a lack of explicit reasoning frameworks and inverse reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: ThinkingVLA uses a unified Mixture-of-Transformers architecture to interleave forward and inverse chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning for subgoal decomposition and spatial anticipation, bridging textual and visual reasoning.
AI.result: ThinkingVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance on simulation and real-world benchmarks, especially excelling in long-horizon manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: ThinkingVLA's unified autoregressive reasoning framework enables it to handle complex reasoning tasks effectively, offering significant improvements over existing models.

title: Theoretical Grounding of Out-Of-Distribution Detection With Reinforcement Learning Optimizer
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in dynamic open-world environments requires a model to continually adapt to evolving data distributions while generalizing to covariate-shifted inputs and rejecting semantic-shifted OOD examples. Most existing OOD detection methods optimize only the current-step objective and do not explicitly account for how post-deployment environment changes affect future OOD behavior. In this paper, we establish a theoretical grounding for dynamic OOD detection using a reinforcement learning (RL)-guided optimizer that explicitly favors updates that reduce the semantic OOD false positive rate over time. We develop a novel augmented optimizer that uses an RL-guided correction term on top of standard gradient descent (GD) and show its improvement over both future-domain generalization and semantic-OOD rejection. We analyze temporal error decomposition in terms of model-change and environment-change generalization errors and develop a new theoretical framework for comparing the generalization errors under both GD and RL-guided optimizers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a reinforcement learning (RL)-guided optimizer for dynamic out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, aiming to better adapt models to changing environments.
AI.motivation: Current OOD detection methods fail to account for evolving data distributions and post-deployment environmental changes that impact future OOD behavior.
AI.method: They propose a novel optimizer based on reinforcement learning that adds a correction term to standard gradient descent, improving semantic OOD rejection in dynamic scenarios.
AI.result: The RL-guided optimizer improves the handling of future-domain generalization and semantic-OOD rejection, outperforming traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical and empirical findings highlight the benefits of using RL-guided optimization for better OOD detection in dynamic, open-world environments.

title: StepGuard: Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration
summary: Web navigation requires agents to follow natural language goals, interact with web pages, and produce accurate answers. While recent advances leverage vision-language models and reinforcement learning, existing methods still suffer from single-step fragility due to reward misalignment and error propagation. To tackle the reward entanglement, we design Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO), which dynamically switches between a navigation-first mode for exploration and an answer-first mode for question-answering to mitigate reward conflict. To calibrate the single-step error, we propose Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR), a mechanism that estimates per-step confidence, triggers reflection only when necessary, and uses contrastive rewards to encourage self-correction to calibrate the single-step inaccuracy. With the above as the main components, we finally develop our StepGuard, a new framework of Guarding Web Navigation via Single-Step Calibration. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves navigation and answer accuracy, setting new state-of-the-art performance on standard web navigation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces StepGuard, a framework enhancing web navigation and Q&A accuracy through robust reward alignment and error correction strategies.
AI.motivation: There is a need to improve agents' ability to navigate the web and follow natural language goals without succumbing to single-step fragility due to reward misalignment and error propagation.
AI.method: The Dynamic Dual-Policy Optimization (DDPO) separates exploration and answer-generation modes to handle reward conflicts, and Confidence-Guided Adaptive Navigation Reflection (CANR) identifies and corrects step-level inaccuracies using per-step confidence estimation and contrastive rewards.
AI.result: The proposed methodology leads to a substantial improvement in navigation and question-answering accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art results on benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: StepGuard demonstrates the effectiveness of combining dynamic policy switching and confidence-guided reflection to address challenges in web navigation systems, particularly in resolving reward misalignment and single-step errors.

title: Perron--Frobenius Operator Matching for Generative Modeling
summary: We introduce Perron--Frobenius Operator Matching (PFOM), a generative framework that matches density evolution via the integral PF operator, subsuming flow, diffusion, and jump models. We prove that among Bregman divergences, only Kullback--Leibler divergence preserves equality between density-level and sample-conditioned objectives, yielding a practical loss equivalent to Koopman path matching. We further develop Nesterov-accelerated training and sampling that stabilize discretization and accelerate convergence. %On Gaussian mixtures and two-moons, PFOM achieves faster KL/$W_2$/MMD decrease and improved wall-clock efficiency with empirical validation. PFOM unifies operator-theoretic identification with modern generative modeling and opens paths to adaptive dictionaries and high-dimensional applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Perron--Frobenius Operator Matching (PFOM), a generative modeling framework based on the integral PF operator, unifying various model types. Novel methods enhance stability and efficiency, shown to outperform baselines in specific tasks.
AI.motivation: To unify operator-theoretic identification with generative modeling and create a framework for density evolution that supports high-dimensional applications.
AI.method: The method is based on matching density evolution using the integral Perron-Frobenius operator and optimizing objectives under Kullback-Leibler divergence. It integrates Nesterov-accelerated techniques for training and sampling.
AI.result: PFOM achieves faster decreases in metrics like KL divergence and improves wall-clock efficiency on benchmarks such as Gaussian mixtures and two-moons datasets.
AI.conclusion: PFOM connects operator theory with modern generative models, providing performance and efficiency advantages and offering potential for broader applications in high-dimensional spaces.

title: Dynamic Rollout Editing for Reducing Overthinking in RL-Trained Reasoning Models
summary: Long-form chain-of-thought reasoning can improve LLM performance on complex tasks, but models often continue generating unnecessary reasoning after a correct answer has emerged. We refer to this behavior as overthinking. We study this phenomenon from the perspective of GRPO-style reinforcement learning (RL) post-training, framing it as a training-time credit-assignment problem rather than merely a decoding-time stopping problem. In rollouts sampled at the onset of GRPO training, we observe that successful trajectories can exhibit a slightly higher degree of overthinking than unsuccessful trajectories for the same prompts. This early imbalance provides a starting point for an undesirable feedback loop: because GRPO assigns sequence-level credit, it cannot distinguish the solution-reaching prefix from the unnecessary continuation that lengthens a successful trajectory. Both receive positive update signal, allowing the initial imbalance to grow into more severe overthinking during training. To address this issue, we introduce Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE), a training-time intervention for successful trajectories that continue thinking after answer emergence. DRE preserves the accepted verified prefix, edits the remaining thinking, and prefers the edited trajectory within the same RL group, weakening the preference signal for unnecessary thinking without penalizing the reasoning needed to reach the answer. Experiments across diverse tasks show the effectiveness of DRE.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how overthinking affects LLMs trained via GRPO reinforcement learning and proposes a training-time intervention called Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE) to reduce unnecessary reasoning.
AI.motivation: LLMs often continue reasoning unnecessarily even after finding correct answers, a problem termed overthinking. This hinders efficiency and prompts the need for better training strategies.
AI.method: The paper identifies overthinking as an issue in GRPO training due to credit-assignment problems and introduces Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE). DRE edits successful reasoning but removes unnecessary parts, altering credit signals during RL training.
AI.result: DRE effectively reduces overthinking by weakening positive updates for unnecessary reasoning without affecting the reasoning required to achieve the correct answers.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE) is a viable solution to mitigate overthinking in LLMs during GRPO training, leading to better performance across diverse tasks.

title: LAGO Policy: Latency-Aware Asynchronous Diffusion Policies with Goal-Directed Collision-Free Planning for Smooth Manipulation
summary: Diffusion-based visuomotor policies deployed with asynchronous inference often exhibit inter-chunk discontinuities and lack explicit mechanisms for obstacle-aware execution, leading to jerky motions and collisions that hinder reliable manipulation in real-world scenes. To address these issues, we propose LAGO Policy, a unified asynchronous action-generation framework that integrates trajectory optimization with diffusion policy for smooth and safe execution. LAGO Policy improves inter-chunk consistency via latency-aware classifier-free guidance conditioning on future actions. It further enables goal-directed collision-free trajectory planning by predicting a task-relevant interaction goal from demonstrations. Finally, spatial-temporal trajectory optimization refines the actions to be executed for low-jerk and feasible motion. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate that LAGO Policy achieves smooth collision-free execution with high task success across challenging manipulation tasks. Project Website: https://lago-policy.github.io/

AI.tldr: LAGO Policy addresses issues in diffusion-based visuomotor policies by integrating trajectory optimization and classifier-free guidance for smooth, safe, and effective task execution.
AI.motivation: To resolve issues such as jerky motions, collisions, and lack of obstacle-awareness in diffusion-based visuomotor policies, hindering their real-world manipulation reliability.
AI.method: LAGO Policy integrates asynchronous action generation, latency-aware classifier-free guidance, goal-directed collision-free trajectory planning, and spatial-temporal trajectory optimization.
AI.result: Extensive real-world experiments show that LAGO Policy ensures smooth collision-free execution and high success rates in challenging manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: LAGO Policy effectively enhances visuomotor policy performance by addressing inter-chunk discontinuities and improving obstacle-awareness.

title: GeneralVLA-2: Geometry-Aware Reconstruction and Governed Memory for Robot Planning
summary: Generalist vision-language-action systems need object-centric 3D evidence and reusable manipulation experience to plan reliable robot trajectories. GeneralVLA provides a hierarchical interface for converting language and RGB-D observations into 3D end-effector paths, but two bottlenecks remain. First, monocular SAM3D-style object reconstruction can hallucinate pose and unseen geometry, while manipulation benefits from stable object shape when calibrated multi-view observations are available. Second, the original KnowledgeBank mainly retrieves semantically similar snippets and appends new knowledge, which makes it difficult to control memory quality, conflicts, confidence, and geometric relevance. To address the first challenge, we introduce GeoFuse-MV3D, a geometry-prior-guided MV-SAM3D reconstruction branch that verifies external geometry cues with input-view masks, applies soft visual-hull support, performs axis-wise refinement, and fuses only geometry while preserving appearance. To address the second challenge, we upgrade KnowledgeBank into a governed long-term memory system with explicit quality, confidence, lifecycle, verifier, and conflict metadata, together with precision-oriented retrieval. Finally, we evaluate the reconstruction branch on GSO-30 and the memory module on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and SWE-Bench Verified; GeoFuse-MV3D improves over the MV-SAM3D baseline by reducing CD and LPIPS by 2.20% and 2.02% while increasing PSNR and SSIM by 2.36% and 1.03%, and KnowledgeBank improves over ReasoningBank by 4.53% on Terminal-Bench SR and 3.73% on SWE-Bench resolve rate, while reducing AS by 4.95% and 5.65%, respectively. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/GeneralVLA-2. Website: https://aigeeksgroup.github.io/GeneralVLA-2.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes GeoFuse-MV3D for improved 3D object reconstruction and upgrades KnowledgeBank for better memory control in vision-language-action systems, achieving notable performance improvements.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address limitations in vision-language-action systems, specifically improving object reconstruction and memory system operations for enhanced robot trajectory planning.
AI.method: GeoFuse-MV3D incorporates geometry-prior-guided reconstruction with multi-view inputs, and KnowledgeBank is upgraded with explicit metadata for precision-oriented retrieval.
AI.result: GeoFuse-MV3D outperforms MV-SAM3D in reconstruction metrics, and KnowledgeBank shows higher task resolution rates with reduced errors compared to ReasoningBank.
AI.conclusion: The proposed solutions effectively enhance 3D reconstruction and memory control, advancing vision-language-action capabilities for robotics.

title: Structural Preservation and the Logical Expressiveness of Graph Neural Networks
summary:
Bridges between graph neural networks (GNNs) and logical formalisms have been established by fixing architectural choices, such as the types of aggregation, combination, and activation functions. These choices define restricted classes of GNNs for which tight correspondences with logical formalisms can be obtained, by showing that logical formulae can be translated into equivalent GNNs and, conversely, that GNNs can be translated into equivalent formulae.
  In this paper we take a semantic perspective by establishing the logical expressiveness of classes of GNN classifiers that are preserved under structural properties: embeddings (extensions), injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms. We show that, for each such property, there exists a fragment of graded modal logic characterising the class of GNNs. In particular, preservation under embeddings, injective homomorphisms, and homomorphisms corresponds to existential graded modal logic, its existential-positive fragment, and existential-positive modal logic, respectively. These results characterise the expressiveness of broad classes of GNNs independently of specific architectural choices, but we also show that each of these classes admits a GNN architecture of the same expressiveness.
  Technically, our approach uses a new well-quasi-order result for trees of bounded height, yielding finite representations of unravelling-invariant classes.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes connections between Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and logical formalisms by analyzing their semantic expressiveness and structural properties, providing logical characterizations for different classes of GNN classifiers.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore the limits of expressiveness of GNNs from a semantic perspective, and to provide a unified framework linking GNN designs with logical formalisms, independent of specific architectural choices.
AI.method: The authors classify GNNs by their preservation properties (embeddings, injective homomorphisms, homomorphisms) and map these classes to specific fragments of graded modal logic. They use a novel well-quasi-ordering result for trees of bounded height to support their theoretical framework.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that embeddings correspond to existential graded modal logic, injective homomorphisms align with its existential-positive fragment, and homomorphisms map to existential-positive modal logic. These logical equivalencies hold regardless of architecture and show that GNN models can achieve the same expressiveness defined by these logical fragments.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that meaningful connections between GNNs and graded modal logic can be leveraged to characterize GNN expressiveness. It also shows that GNN architectures can be designed to match the expressiveness dictated by these logical classes, providing a semantic foundation for GNN design.

title: ReRAM-aware Model Finetuning addressing I-V Non-linearity and Retention Errors
comment: 11 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables, with appendix (5 pages, 9 figures)
summary: Traditional CPU, GPU, and NPU architectures are increasingly limited by the von Neumann bottleneck. While In-Memory Computing (IMC) using ReRAM crossbar arrays offers a high-density, energy-efficient alternative, its practical deployment is constrained through their non-idealities. Existing hardware-aware training frameworks often require training from scratch, which is computationally prohibitive for modern large-scale models. In this work, we propose a finetuning-based hardware-aware training algorithm that enables robust DNN deployment on ReRAM with minimal training overhead. Our approach mitigates I-V non-linearity by applying a range-shrunk sinh transformation and incorporates retention errors directly into a regularization loss during the finetuning process. We evaluate our framework across models and tasks such as image classification and question-answering (QA). Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves similar accuracy on large-scale models like ResNet18 and DeiT-Tiny as the base model. In-case of ImageNet for MobileNetV3 families the technique has only less than 2% accuracy degradation. Further, applying the technique on the SQuAD v2 dataset results in only 1 point degradation of F-1 score.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hardware-aware training algorithm to enable deep neural network (DNN) deployment on ReRAM by addressing hardware issues like non-linearity and retention errors, achieving minimal accuracy degradation.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations posed by von Neumann bottleneck and non-idealities of ReRAM crossbars, and to reduce computational overhead associated with training large-scale models from scratch for hardware-aware deployment.
AI.method: A finetuning-based hardware-aware training algorithm that mitigates non-linearity using a sinh transformation and incorporates retention errors into the loss function during finetuning.
AI.result: The method achieves near-baseline accuracy across various models and tasks. For example, less than 2% degradation in accuracy for MobileNetV3 on ImageNet and 1-point F-1 degradation on the SQuAD v2 dataset.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers an efficient, robust method for deploying DNNs on ReRAM, achieving competitive accuracy with minimal computational overhead.

title: ChLogic: Evaluating Robustness of Logical Reasoning in Chinese Expressions
summary: Large language models perform increasingly well on standardized logical reasoning benchmarks, but whether this ability remains robust beyond English is unclear. We introduce ChLogic, an English--Chinese aligned benchmark that tests whether models preserve logical reasoning performance when the same latent logical structure is expressed in English and diverse Chinese surface realizations. Built from formal logical templates, the benchmark contains three data sets: (i) the General aligned set, derived from 60 General Propositions across nine template families; (ii) the Difficult aligned set, derived from 40 Difficult Problems; and (iii) the Chinese-only set, covering 15 language-specific phenomenon types. Each aligned item pairs one English reference expression with five Chinese realizations. Experiments on Qwen3, Ministral, and GLM models reveal a persistent English--Chinese performance gap. Back-translation from standard Chinese into English often improves performance on the General aligned set, but produces mixed effects on the Difficult aligned set, where Qwen3-32B and GLM-5.1 perform worse after translation. These results indicate that Chinese surface realization, translation artifacts, and model-specific behavior jointly affect multilingual logical reasoning. Overall, ChLogic provides a useful stress test for the robustness of multilingual reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ChLogic, a benchmark evaluating logical reasoning consistency in English and Chinese expressions, revealing persistent performance gaps in multilingual models.
AI.motivation: To assess whether language models can robustly handle logical reasoning across both English and Chinese language representations.
AI.method: Developed ChLogic, a benchmark from formal logical templates, consisting of three datasets: General aligned, Difficult aligned, and Chinese-only sets, with experiments conducted on models like Qwen3, Ministral, and GLM.
AI.result: Models show a consistent performance gap between English and Chinese; back-translation improves General aligned set performance but has mixed impacts on the Difficult aligned set.
AI.conclusion: Chinese surface realization, translation artifacts, and model-specific traits significantly influence multilingual logical reasoning; ChLogic offers an effective tool for stress-testing models.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Flow-Based Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) combine vision-language backbones with expressive generative action heads trained via flow matching on large-scale robotic datasets. Despite their strong empirical performance in robotic manipulation, VLAs lack mechanisms to quantify confidence in their predictions and to detect when their actions may be unreliable. This presents a critical limitation for real-world deployment in non-stationary environments, where models inevitably encounter scenarios outside their pretraining distribution and may fail without warning. To address this, we derive an efficient method for quantifying epistemic uncertainty in flow-matching models by leveraging velocity-field disagreement (VFD) across a small ensemble. We successfully use this uncertainty estimate for failure detection during deployment and active fine-tuning of flow-based VLAs. To this end, we propose SAVE, a framework for uncertainty-guided active multitask fine-tuning that reduces the number of costly expert demonstrations required to adapt VLAs to new tasks. Through extensive experiments on the LIBERO benchmark, we demonstrate that VFD yields better-calibrated uncertainty estimates predictive of downstream performance, that VFD achieves strong performance in detecting failures, and that uncertainty-guided data acquisition with SAVE requires at least 22% fewer samples than baselines. In summary, our work shows that quantifying epistemic uncertainty in flow-based VLAs improves both failure awareness and adaptation. Project website: tum-lsy.github.io/uq_vla/.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SAVE, a framework for uncertainty quantification in vision-language-action models (VLAs), enhancing failure detection and efficiency in adapting models to new tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of VLAs lacking mechanisms for quantifying confidence in their predictions, crucial for deployment in real-world, non-stationary environments.
AI.method: The authors derive an approach for epistemic uncertainty quantification using velocity-field disagreement (VFD) in flow-matching models, applied for failure detection and active fine-tuning.
AI.result: Through experiments on the LIBERO benchmark, the proposed VFD method improves uncertainty calibration, enhances failure detection, and reduces required expert demonstration samples by at least 22%.
AI.conclusion: Quantifying epistemic uncertainty in flow-based VLAs improves failure awareness and facilitates efficient adaptation, making the models more reliable for real-world applications.

title: SPHINX: First Explain, Then Explore
summary: Generating adversarial driving scenarios is critical for evaluating and improving autonomous vehicle decision-making systems in simulation. Recent approaches, such as ChatScene and LLM-Attacker, rely primarily on the prior knowledge of Large Language Models and Vision-Language Models to generate driving scenarios procedurally. We argue that adversarial scenes should be generated based on the failure diagnosis (e.g., indecisiveness, multi-frame inconsistency) of the driving policy to specifically address the policy's weaknesses instead of relying on prior assumptions. In this paper, we propose SPHINX, a closed-loop framework for adversarial scenario synthesis guided by a simple principle: first explain, then explore. Beyond blindly exploring the scenario space, SPHINX leverages explainable artificial intelligence methods to analyze the policy, identifying key visual concepts and their influence on policy outputs, and the uncertainty of the decisions. Given the interpretable evidence extracted from the policy's own decision process, we use a vision language model to rationalize and criticize failure modes of the current policy. These critics are then used to generate targeted adversarial scenarios for policy retraining and improvement. We demonstrate that SPHINX can highlight an interpretable account of policy failures while other adversarial scene generation cannot. Across the evaluated benchmarks and test suites, SPHINX can be applied to diverse state-of-the-art autonomous vehicle architectures and yields consistent robustness improvements over existing scenario-generation methods.

AI.tldr: The SPHINX framework addresses flaws in autonomous vehicle decision-making policies by generating adversarial driving scenarios based on failure explanations.
AI.motivation: The need to improve evaluation and robustness of autonomous vehicle decision-making systems using failure-informed adversarial scenarios.
AI.method: SPHINX combines explainable AI methods to analyze policy failures and vision-language models to synthesize targeted adversarial scenarios for policy retraining.
AI.result: SPHINX identifies interpretable policy failures and outperforms existing methods in generating scenarios that improve robustness across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SPHINX enhances autonomous vehicle system robustness by integrating explainability and targeted adversarial scenario generation.

title: MathVis-Fine: Aligning Visual Supervision with Necessity via Progressive Dependency-Guided Training for Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has extended from purely linguistic domains to multimodal scenarios; however, existing approaches often treat visual inputs as homogeneous or auxiliary signals, failing to capture the intricate and sample-specific dependencies between text and images in mathematical problem-solving. This gives rise to two core issues: first, the supervisory signals for visual content are generalized and coarse-grained, lacking adaptation to the actual necessity of visual information in each sample; second, training feedback becomes inaccurate when visual rewards are uniformly applied without distinguishing the complementary relationships among inputs. These limitations hinder models from achieving precise multimodal reasoning. In this work, we propose a framework for modeling fine-grained visual dependencies in mathematical reasoning. We first construct the MathVis-Fine dataset, augmenting fine-grained visual annotations with visual dependency ratings. Building upon this dataset, we introduce a two-stage progressive visual enhancement training paradigm that balances answer correctness rewards and visual grounding rewards according to the intrinsic visual dependency level of each sample, thereby mitigating reward bias and improving supervision accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the MathVis-Fine framework effectively enhances visual perception progressively based on visual dependency, offering a more precise training framework for multimodal mathematical reasoning. We will release the dataset upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework merging fine-grained vision and text for mathematical problem-solving, resolving limitations in multimodal reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches struggle to model the intricate, sample-specific dependencies between text and visuals, leading to coarse-grained supervision and uniform feedback, limiting precise multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: The authors developed the MathVis-Fine dataset with visual dependency ratings and proposed a two-stage progressive training approach balancing answer correctness and visual grounding rewards based on visual dependency levels.
AI.result: Experiments show improved visual perception and reasoning accuracy using the MathVis-Fine framework.
AI.conclusion: The framework captures fine-grained visual dependencies in multimodal reasoning and addresses reward bias, advancing precise mathematical problem-solving.

title: Multi-Adapter PPO: A Cross-Attention Enhanced Wavelength Selection Framework for LIBS Quantitative Analysis
summary: Laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) quantitative analysis faces critical challenges in wavelength selection due to high-dimensional spectral data and the fundamental trade-off between prediction accuracy and feature efficiency. This paper presents a novel Multi-Adapter PPO framework that transforms wavelength selection into a reinforcement learning problem, leveraging cross-attention mechanisms and multiple specialized adapters to capture complex spectral relationships. Our approach outperforms traditional Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) by an average of 28.4\% in comprehensive score and 45.2\% in prediction accuracy across steel and coal datasets. The proposed method demonstrates superior performance in balancing prediction accuracy with feature efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art results in LIBS quantitative analysis while maintaining interpretability and computational efficiency. We released our code and dataset here: https://github.com/Hflying/MAPPO

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Multi-Adapter PPO framework to improve wavelength selection for LIBS analysis, leveraging reinforcement learning and cross-attention mechanisms to achieve superior accuracy and feature efficiency.
AI.motivation: Wavelength selection in LIBS quantitative analysis is a challenging task due to high-dimensional spectral data and the compromise required between prediction accuracy and feature efficiency.
AI.method: The authors propose the Multi-Adapter PPO framework, which uses reinforcement learning with cross-attention mechanisms and specialized adapters to model complex spectral relationships for wavelength selection.
AI.result: The approach achieved a 28.4% improvement in comprehensive score and a 45.2% increase in prediction accuracy compared to Particle Swarm Optimization, tested on steel and coal datasets.
AI.conclusion: This framework establishes a new state-of-the-art in LIBS quantitative analysis, effectively balancing prediction accuracy and feature efficiency while maintaining computational and interpretive advantages. The authors have also made their code and dataset publicly available.

title: Learning task-specific subspaces via interventional post-training of speech foundation models
comment: Accepted to Interspeech 2026; 6 pages (4 main body), 2 figures
summary: Speech foundation models, pre-trained on large corpora of unlabelled speech data, produce general-purpose representations which are useful across tasks. However, these representations encode information about salient speech variables in a distributed manner, while downstream speech tasks rely on only some of this variability.
