These findings indicate that while delta reformulation is a powerful inductive bias for neural networks, its efficacy is model- and horizon-dependent.

AI.tldr: This paper explores using delta-based reformulation (predicting load changes instead of absolute values) to improve short-term electricity load forecasting using deep learning models.
AI.motivation: Accurately predicting short-term electricity load is crucial due to non-stationarity caused by weather, calendar effects, and evolving consumption, and existing models often fail to address this explicitly.
AI.method: Inspired by ARIMA differencing techniques, the paper proposes training models to predict load deltas between consecutive time steps and reconstructing forecasts using the last observed load.
AI.result: The delta-based approach significantly improves hour-ahead forecasting accuracy, reducing MAPE by over 50%, especially benefiting LSTM and Transformer models for day-ahead forecasting.
AI.conclusion: Delta-based reformulation offers accuracy benefits, particularly for deep sequence models, but its effectiveness varies by model and prediction horizon.

title: Fixed-Point Reasoners: Stable and Adaptive Deep Looped Transformers
summary: Looped architectures provide an inductive bias toward learning step-by-step procedures for tasks that require compositional reasoning. The number of effective layers reached by looping determines the quality of the solution these models find. Like deep architectures, looped architectures are prone to a signal propagation problem induced by depth as the halting decision is postponed. In this paper, we address this signal propagation issue using pre-norm layers and residual scaling. Building on these architectural modifications, we propose FPRM, a Transformer-based Fixed-Point Reasoning Model that uses fixed-point convergence as an end-to-end halting mechanism in a looped architecture. We show that fixed-point halting allows FPRM to adapt its compute to task difficulty. FPRM is effective on common reasoning benchmarks, namely Sudoku, Maze, state-tracking, and ARC-AGI.

AI.tldr: The paper presents FPRM, a Transformer-based model using fixed-point convergence as an end-to-end halting mechanism to enhance performance in compositional reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the signal propagation problem inherent in looped architectures during step-by-step reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The authors employ pre-norm layers, residual scaling, and a fixed-point convergence mechanism to develop the Fixed-Point Reasoning Model (FPRM).
AI.result: FPRM is shown to be effective on tasks requiring compositional reasoning, such as Sudoku, Maze, state-tracking, and ARC-AGI benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The use of fixed-point halting in looped architectures allows the model to adapt computational effort to the complexity of tasks, enhancing reasoning capabilities.

title: Confusion-Aware Transfer Teacher Curriculum Learning Framework: Disentangling Scoring and Pacing Effects
comment: Accepted at International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) GlobalSouthML Workshop (2026)
summary: Curriculum learning couples two design choices, how samples are scored by difficulty and how harder samples are paced into training, making it difficult to attribute observed gains to either component. We disentangle these factors with two evaluation protocols: stage-wise test subsets that validate scoring functions independently of curriculum training, and a baseline that applies the same pacing schedule to randomly ordered data. Within the Transfer Teacher framework (TTF), we use these protocols to evaluate a confusion-aware difficulty score that considers both correct-class confidence and the probability distribution over incorrect classes. On CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 and VGG-16, the proposed score produces model-interpretable difficulty rankings that align with human intuition. However, at full data, neither curriculum nor anti-curriculum ordering improves accuracy over standard training, indicating that improving the scoring function alone is insufficient to overcome the known failure modes of curriculum learning in TTF. In contrast, We find that confusion-aware curriculum ordering result in consistent data-efficiency benefits, outperforming random ordering by up to 8.7% points at the 20% data regime, suggesting the potential of TTF as a data-efficient training method.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates curriculum learning by disentangling scoring functions from training pacing, proposing a confusion-aware difficulty score evaluated within the Transfer Teacher framework.
AI.motivation: The researchers aim to address the difficulty in attributing improvements in curriculum learning to scoring functions or pacing schedules, while also exploring data efficiency in training.
AI.method: They use stage-wise test subsets and a baseline random ordering for evaluation, applying a new confusion-aware difficulty score based on confidence and incorrect-class probabilities.
AI.result: The study finds that while curriculum ordering does not enhance accuracy with full data, confusion-aware ordering improves data efficiency, outperforming random ordering in limited data regimes by up to 8.7% points.
AI.conclusion: Improving scoring functions alone is insufficient for full-data scenarios, but confusion-aware curriculum shows promise for data-efficient training methods, especially in restricted data settings.

title: LiveStarPro: Proactive Streaming Video Understanding with Hierarchical Memory for Long-Horizon Streams
summary: Despite the remarkable progress of Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs), current online architectures still struggle to simultaneously process continuous video streams, decide autonomously when to respond, and preserve long-horizon contextual memory. These obstacles undermine real-time responsiveness and cause severe forgetting throughout prolonged interactions. In this work, we introduce LiveStarPro, a live streaming assistant that is designed for proactive video understanding over long-horizon streams. The design of LiveStarPro rests on three complementary components. The first component is Streaming Verification Decoding (SVeD), an inference framework that identifies the appropriate response timing through single-pass perplexity verification, thereby eliminating the dependency on explicit silence tokens. The second component is Streaming Causal Attention Masks (SCAM), a training strategy that enforces incremental video-language alignment over variable-length streams. The third component is Tree-Structured Hierarchical Memory (TSHM), a recursive memory architecture that organizes evicted historical information into event chains and consequently enables efficient retrieval from effectively unbounded video streams. To facilitate a comprehensive evaluation under realistic online conditions, we further present OmniStarPro, a large-scale benchmark that spans 15 diverse real-world scenarios and that extends to hour-scale streams for the assessment of long-term recall. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LiveStarPro consistently surpasses existing methods, attaining a 28.9% improvement in semantic correctness and an 18.2% reduction in timing error, while its streaming key-value cache further yields a 1.58x inference speedup over the same model without caching. The model and the code are publicly available at https://github.com/sotayang/LiveStarPro.

AI.tldr: LiveStarPro is a video streaming assistant designed to improve real-time video understanding, response timing, and long-term memory over continuous video streams, introduced alongside a new benchmark OmniStarPro.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in Video-LLMs such as handling continuous video streams, determining optimal response timing autonomously, and retaining long-horizon contextual memory.
AI.method: LiveStarPro employs three components: SVeD for response timing, SCAM for training with incremental video-language alignment, and TSHM for organizing long-horizon contextual memory in continuous interactions.
AI.result: LiveStarPro surpasses existing methods with a 28.9% improvement in semantic correctness, an 18.2% reduction in timing errors, and a 1.58x inference speedup due to an optimized streaming cache.
AI.conclusion: LiveStarPro significantly advances real-time video understanding, efficiency, and long-term memory handling in Video-LLMs, offering promising applications and outperforming state-of-the-art models.

title: EvolveNav: Proactive Preflection and Self-Evolving Memory for Zero-Shot Object Goal Navigation
summary: Zero-Shot Object-Goal Navigation (ZS-OGN) requires embodied agents to explore and locate target objects without any prior training. To this end, recent methods leverage foundation models. But they typically rely on static priors and lack adaptation, which leads to repeated errors and costly trial and error. In this paper, we propose a self-evolving ZS-OGN framework that enables continuous test-time improvement. Specifically, we build an agentic rule memory by extracting actionable knowledge from past trajectories. Then, we propose a retrieval strategy based on upper confidence bound, selecting effective rules by balancing semantic relevance and historical success. In addition, we introduce a memory-guided preflection module that forecasts potential outcomes before action, reducing inefficient exploration. Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing zero-shot baselines, achieving a 10.1\% improvement in success rate with fewer unnecessary steps.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a continuous self-evolving framework for zero-shot object-goal navigation (ZS-OGN) to improve exploration efficiency and success rate.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of static priors and lack of adaptability in current ZS-OGN approaches, which result in errors and inefficiencies.
AI.method: Develop an agentic rule memory to learn from past trajectories, use an upper confidence bound-based retrieval strategy for rule selection, and introduce a memory-guided preflection module to predict outcomes before action.
AI.result: Achieves a 10.1% improvement in success rate with fewer unnecessary steps compared to existing zero-shot baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates improved adaptation and efficiency in ZS-OGN, showing potential for better zero-shot navigation in real-world scenarios.

title: A fairness-aware extension of Stochastic Multicriteria Acceptability Analysis for ranking
summary: Fairness has become a central concern in ranking problems involving individuals or social groups, particularly under the Responsible Artificial Intelligence agenda. In Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis, Stochastic Multicriteria Acceptability Analysis (SMAA) provides a robust framework for handling uncertainty and incomplete preference information, but it does not explicitly address fairness in the resulting rankings. This paper proposes SMAA-Fair, a fairness-aware extension of SMAA for ranking problems. The approach reweights the simulated rankings generated by SMAA according to their level of group fairness, so that fairer rankings contribute more strongly to the acceptability indices and central weights vector. The framework is independent of the aggregation model and can incorporate different fairness metrics. In this study, Statistical Parity, normalized discounted Kullback--Leibler divergence (rKL) and normalized discounted cumulative Kullback--Leibler divergence (nDKL) are adopted. Rankings are derived from the fairness-adjusted acceptability matrix using expected ranking and maximum acceptability ranking. We also derive the central weight according to the degree of fairness in the obtained rankings. Numerical experiments with synthetic and real data show that SMAA-Fair improves the representation of protected groups among favourable ranking positions, while preserving robustness to preference uncertainty.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SMAA-Fair, an extension of SMAA that integrates fairness into rankings by reweighting rankings based on fairness metrics, showing improved group representation while retaining preference uncertainty robustness.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of explicit fairness considerations in traditional SMAA for ranking problems, particularly important under the Responsible Artificial Intelligence agenda.
AI.method: Develop SMAA-Fair by reweighting simulated rankings based on fairness metrics (e.g., Statistical Parity, rKL, nDKL), and derive rankings via fairness-adjusted acceptability matrices and central weights.
AI.result: Numerical experiments with synthetic and real data demonstrate that SMAA-Fair enhances protected group representation in top rankings while maintaining robustness to preference uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: SMAA-Fair effectively incorporates fairness into ranking problems, balancing fairness and robustness, making it a valuable extension to SMAA under ethical AI frameworks.

title: MaineCoon: Pursuing A Real-Time Audio-Visual Social World Model
summary: As an increasing majority of global video content is consumed on social platforms for interactive social purposes, video generation models built for social worlds are important but largely overlooked by previous studies. In this work, we define the position of social world models and build a prototype model as the first step towards this goal. While previous world models successfully simulate physical environments or gaming world exploration, they remain fundamentally detached from human-centric social dynamics. To bridge this gap as the first step to social world models, we present MaineCoon, the first real-time audio-visual autoregressive model that has 22B parameters and is capable of real-time streaming generation and sub-second interaction, with a record-breaking frame rate of up to 47.5 FPS, on a single GPU. To the best of our knowledge, MaineCoon is also the first real-time audio-visual generation model specifically optimized for social-interactive applications. To enable efficient and stable training, we introduce several novel techniques into MaineCoon, including self-resampling, cross-modal representation alignment, domain-aware preference optimization, and reinforced online-policy distillation (ROPD). We also design the first agentic streaming inference framework that supports thousand-second-scale or even longer generation while mitigating drift with agentic cache management and prompt planing. These innovations significantly accelerate training while optimizing real-time inference performance. We believe this work not only sets a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance benchmark for high-quality, low-latency, and long-horizon audio-visual autoregressive models, but also points out the paradigm shift desired for next-generation AI-native social platforms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MaineCoon, a real-time audio-visual generation model specifically tailored for social-interactive applications, achieving breakthrough performance.
AI.motivation: Global video consumption on social platforms has risen, creating a need for video generation models optimized for social contexts. Traditional models lack the focus on interactive human-centric social dynamics.
AI.method: The authors developed MaineCoon, a 22B parameter real-time audio-visual autoregressive model. It employs techniques such as self-resampling, cross-modal representation alignment, and reinforced online-policy distillation for effective training and performance.
AI.result: MaineCoon achieves a record-breaking frame rate of 47.5 FPS on a single GPU, with efficient and stable training innovations, making it the first SOTA real-time model for social interaction.
AI.conclusion: This work pioneers the concept of social world models tailored for next-gen interactive social platforms and sets a new benchmark for real-time audio-visual generation.

title: Blind Recovery of Latent Domains via Unsupervised Symmetry Discovery
summary: Primary motivation in blind inverse problems is to recover signals of interest from corrupted observations without knowing the obfuscating mechanism. Blind deconvolution is a prominent approach when the corruption is convolutional, but it is not applicable when general linear transformations obfuscate the domain structure. In this work, we propose an unsupervised framework for recovering latent domains and signals by discovering symmetries of the data distribution. Our framework models observations as linear measurements of signals sampled from a latent random field, and optimizes a shallow group-convolutional network by imposing stationarity and locality regularization at the model output. The model learns a latent symmetry action and an appropriate filter, thereby mapping unstructured observations to a symmetry-based representation that reveals latent signals. Experiments on stochastic processes, Ising models, shuffled and bit-scrambled images, and neural recordings show that the method recovers latent domains and signals from unstructured observations, suggesting symmetry discovery as a new direction for unsupervised structure learning and blind inverse problems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an unsupervised framework to recover signals from corrupted data using symmetry discovery rather than traditional blind deconvolution.
AI.motivation: Recovering signals from corrupted data without prior knowledge of the obfuscating mechanism is challenging, especially when general linear transformations are involved.
AI.method: The paper proposes optimizing a shallow group-convolutional network, leveraging stationarity and locality regularization to discover latent symmetries, domains, and signals.
AI.result: The method successfully recovers latent domains and signals in various experiments, including stochastic processes, images, and neural recordings.
AI.conclusion: Symmetry discovery is a promising approach for unsupervised structure learning and tackling blind inverse problems.

title: PARSE: Provenance-Aware Retrieval Sanitization for Professional Domain LLM Agents
comment: 7 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Under submission at EMNLP 2026 Industry Track
summary: Prompt injection defenses evaluated on synthetic benchmarks do not generalize to real enterprise documents, which are longer, denser, and interleave legitimate authority language with factual content. We demonstrate this gap with a real-document benchmark of 122 tasks across five professional domains (financial, legal, medical, scientific, DevOps) using actual SEC filings, Federal Register rules, PubMed abstracts, arXiv papers, and GitHub postmortems. Paraphrasing, the strongest defense on synthetic benchmarks, shows no statistically significant attack success rate reduction on real documents (p=0.500) while degrading utility from 91.8% to 82.8%. We introduce PARSE (Provenance-Aware Retrieval Sanitization), a domain-aware, fact-preserving sanitization pipeline that classifies each sentence by injection likelihood, extracts structured facts before rewriting, and verifies fact preservation via a consistency-checking loop. A directiveness gate routes 59% of real enterprise documents to a lightweight path, concentrating computational cost on high-risk documents. PARSE achieves 15.6% attack success rate -- a 38% reduction versus the 25.4% baseline -- at 86.9% utility, the only condition that is both statistically significant (p=0.014, adequately powered) and maintains near-baseline utility. Practitioners should evaluate defenses on domain-matched real documents, not synthetic proxies.

AI.tldr: Synthetic benchmarks fail to evaluate prompt injection defenses effectively on real enterprise documents; authors introduce PARSE to address this gap.
AI.motivation: Enterprise documents are significantly more complex than synthetic benchmarks, requiring novel defenses against prompt injection attacks.
AI.method: The paper uses a real-document benchmark with 122 tasks across five domains, introduces PARSE as a domain-aware sanitization pipeline, and evaluates its performance against synthetic defenses.
AI.result: PARSE reduces attack success rate by 38% while maintaining utility at 86.9%, outperforming other approaches in real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: Defenses should be tested on domain-specific real documents rather than synthetic proxies; PARSE is an effective approach to mitigate prompt injection attacks.

title: Million-scale multimodal pollen microscopy with expert-guided foundation models
comment: 31 pages, 5 main figures, supplementary information included. Submitted to Scientific Reports
summary: Automated pollen identification from microscopy remains a bottleneck in aerobiology, palaeoecology and biodiversity monitoring, because scalable systems must generalise across specimen preparation, scanner settings and geographic origins while retaining palynological interpretability. To address this gap, we present a million-scale multimodal pollen microscopy resource, Pollen AI Atlas, assembled from pure-species whole-slide bright-field images spanning four geographic origins, four scanner settings and 46 taxon labels across 31 botanical families. Seeded by one manually selected exemplar per source slide, token-level mining and filtering produced 1,511,390 released grain detections with 99.6\% proposal precision in expert-curated test regions. Each detection was paired with machine-generated grain-level morphological captions from five open-weight vision-language models, guided by expert-verified palynological anchors, yielding structured descriptions of aperture systems, wall ornamentation, shape and size. Among the evaluated models, Gemma4 provided the most controlled primary caption set, combining tight length control, no leakage and the strongest text-retrieval performance. Baseline benchmarks with frozen visual features reached 88.16\% top-1 accuracy, while cross-regional retrieval showed that caption-derived text embeddings remained robust when image similarity degraded (mAP@20 0.811 versus 0.262). Released data, annotations, captions, splits, code, and weights provide a benchmark for pollen recognition, cross-regional domain adaptation and domain-specific multimodal microscopy learning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Pollen AI Atlas, a massive multimodal microscopy resource for automated pollen identification across diverse conditions and regions.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenges in scalable automated pollen identification, including generalization across specimen preparation, scanner settings, and geographic origins while maintaining interpretability for palynologists.
AI.method: The authors curated over 1.5 million pollen grain detections from visual data spanning multiple regions and device setups, paired these with expert-guided machine-generated morphological captions, and used benchmark evaluations to test the system's accuracy and adaptability.
AI.result: The dataset achieved a 99.6% precision in key regions, enabled detailed morphological descriptions of pollen grains, and demonstrated high baseline classification accuracy (88.16%) and robust cross-regional adaptability in text-retrieval tasks.
AI.conclusion: The released dataset and resources establish a robust benchmark for pollen recognition, cross-regional domain transfer, and multimodal learning in microscopy, contributing to fields like aerobiology and biodiversity monitoring.

title: Continual Self-Improvement with Lightweight Experiential Latent Memories
summary: Large language models achieve strong reasoning performance by scaling inference-time compute, yet remain fundamentally stateless, discarding the rich, self-produced reasoning traces generated during this process. We investigate whether models can instead learn online from this experience, converting transient computation (reasoning traces) into persistent reusable knowledge, and without external supervision or access to future data. We show that In-Context Learning (ICL) over raw reasoning traces fails to generalize, reflecting a fundamental limitation of token-level reuse: individual traces lack the abstraction needed for transfer, even after refinement (e.g. self-reflection). In contrast, drawing inspiration from recent works on unsupervised reinforcement learning, we find that lightweight per-instance training with self-generated test-time signals (majority voting) as rewards yields substantial gains, often surpassing full-dataset offline training, motivating a shift from raw traces to learned latent representations. Building on this insight, we propose an online method that distills inference-time compute spent on encountered problems into compact modular latent memories capturing the underlying reasoning structure. These memories are stored and retrieved for future inputs, enabling continual improvement while avoiding catastrophic forgetting through modular design. Importantly, our method is highly efficient, parametrized as extremely lightweight soft prompt memories (~0.001% of model parameters) and trained with only a few gradient steps, yet achieving performance competitive with full parametric updates and offline training. Across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, our approach significantly outperforms zero-shot and raw data ICL baselines, while transferring effectively across datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the potential for large language models (LLMs) to convert reasoning traces from online computations into reusable, persistent knowledge via a new lightweight method, surpassing traditional raw traces and offline training approaches.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address a fundamental limitation in LLMs: their inability to retain and reuse knowledge from reasoning traces generated during inference, resulting in under-explored opportunities for continual learning and improvement.
AI.method: The proposed method draws from unsupervised reinforcement learning and introduces lightweight, per-instance training with self-generated rewards (like majority voting). It distills inference-time compute into modular latent memories stored as soft prompts, retrievable for future use.
AI.result: The method achieves substantial performance improvements over zero-shot baselines, raw trace ICL, and often even offline training, on challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks. It also demonstrates effective transfer across datasets while avoiding catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the potential of transforming transient reasoning traces into persistent latent representations through efficient online training. This enables scalable continual learning in LLMs, with significant performance benefits and promising generalization abilities.

title: Non-Autoregressive Minimum Bayes' Risk Decoding for Fast Speech Recognition
summary: Non-autoregressive (NAR) decoding generates output tokens in parallel, making speech recognition faster than autoregressive decoding, which generates them sequentially from left to right. However, the recognition performance is degraded because NAR decoding cannot resolve uncertainty by conditioning on previously generated tokens. To address this issue, we propose a novel NAR decoding framework based on minimum Bayes' risk (MBR) decoding, termed NAR-MBR decoding, that maximizes the expected utility calculated from samples drawn from the output probability of an NAR model rather than maximizing the output probability. Notably, by leveraging the nature of NAR models, multiple samples are obtained efficiently with a single forward computation. Our experiments across LibriSpeech, Switchboard, AMI, and web presentation corpus demonstrated that our NAR-MBR decoding outperformed previous NAR decoding and ran faster than AR decoding.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel framework for non-autoregressive decoding in speech recognition, called NAR-MBR decoding, to improve recognition performance while maintaining speed advantages.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the degradation in recognition performance in non-autoregressive decoding caused by its inability to resolve uncertainty using previously generated tokens.
AI.method: The paper proposes NAR-MBR decoding, a framework that maximizes expected utility by using samples efficiently drawn from the output probability of an NAR model with a single forward computation.
AI.result: The proposed method outperformed previous NAR decoding approaches and executed faster than autoregressive decoding in experiments conducted on various datasets, including LibriSpeech and Switchboard.
AI.conclusion: NAR-MBR decoding improves speech recognition performance while maintaining the computational efficiency of non-autoregressive models, making it a promising alternative to autoregressive decoding.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Atlas-Based 3D Asset Segmentation for Interactive Content Workflows
summary: Segmenting 3D assets into meaningful regions remains challenging, especially when segmentation criteria are application-dependent and require user control. We present a human-in-the-loop pipeline for generating a segmented 2D parameterized atlas from a 3D model for interactive media, game, and XR content workflows. Our method first selects a compact set of rendered views using a greedy set cover strategy over sampled surface points, and then supports interactive segmentation of these views with SAM~2 and Label Studio. The resulting masks are back-projected onto the model's UV parameterization to produce a unified segmented atlas that supports downstream production tasks such as segment-wise material assignment, style transfer, and semantic labeling. We assess the pipeline through a demonstration-based technical evaluation on eight cultural heritage objects. The results show that the approach can generate usable segmented atlases across diverse geometries while revealing recurring sources of manual correction, particularly fine structures, cavities, and weak appearance boundaries.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a human-in-the-loop process for segmenting 3D models into meaningful parts for downstream applications like gaming and XR.
AI.motivation: Segmenting 3D assets remains difficult due to varying application criteria and the need for user control.
AI.method: The method involves selecting key rendered views using a greedy set cover strategy, then enabling interactive segmentation with SAM~2 and Label Studio. Masks are back-projected to create a segmented UV atlas.
AI.result: Using eight cultural heritage objects, the pipeline demonstrated its versatility and identified recurring issues such as fine structures and weak boundaries.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively creates segmented atlases but highlights areas for improvement, including dealing with fine details and boundary weaknesses.

title: PIVOT: Bridging Black-Scholes Implied-Volatility and Price Objectives via Differentiable Jäckel Operator
summary:
Modern option-learning systems operate in two coordinates: price space, where markets quote and no-arbitrage constraints are most naturally enforced, and implied volatility (IV) space, where volatility surfaces are smoothed, regularized, and evaluated. The bottleneck is interface, not approximation: Jäckel's seminal "Let's Be Rational" (LBR) solver already inverts the Black-Scholes price to machine precision efficiently. What is missing is a differentiable layer that preserves LBR in the forward pass and avoids backpropagating through its branch logic. Such a layer must also confront the unavoidable singularity of the inverse map in the low-vega regime, where the sensitivity 1/vega diverges as vega -> 0.
  We close this gap with PIVOT, the Price-Implied-Volatility Objective Translator. PIVOT keeps the LBR forward pass intact and supplies the backward pass by implicit differentiation through the smooth Black-Scholes/Black-76 price map, with an explicit gating contract: invalid domains return NaN, well-conditioned rows receive the exact 1/vega gradient, and low-vega rows are attenuated rather than silently regularized. On a single H100, a fused Triton kernel reaches 1.79e9 IV/s at machine precision (9.3e-14 max relative error vs. the reference C solver); end-to-end label generation sustains 48.9M/s on synthetic chains and 16.6M/s on SPX OptionMetrics. In a HyperIV-style one-day reproduction on SPX, PIVOT-augmented objectives Pareto-dominate the baselines, reducing held-out price MAE by up to 43.4% and the strongest three-seed gated objective improving price MAE by 38.8% and IV MAE by 21.3% jointly; cross-asset results on RUT, VIX, and NDX show directional price-MAE gains of 40.1%, 24.2%, and 16.7%, while an ungated IV-roundtrip control collapses to a degenerate near-zero surface, confirming the gate as a correctness contract rather than a tuning knob.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PIVOT, a system enhancing option-learning by efficiently inverting Black-Scholes prices with machine precision while handling low-vega regime issues and providing better gradient backpropagation.
AI.motivation: Existing option-learning systems face challenges in seamlessly transitioning between price space and implied volatility (IV) space, particularly in ensuring smooth gradients and avoiding complexities from singularities in the low-vega regime.
AI.method: The authors developed a differentiable layer, PIVOT, which maintains LBR's efficiency during the forward pass and achieves smooth backward gradients using implicit differentiation. It utilizes a gating mechanism to handle invalid or low-vega domains appropriately.
AI.result: PIVOT achieves remarkable speed and precision (1.79 billion IV calculations/s with minimal errors) and significantly improves error metrics (up to 43.4% lower price MAE) in SPX experiments. Cross-asset tests also show consistent directional price-MAE reductions.
AI.conclusion: PIVOT addresses critical bottlenecks in option-learning systems, demonstrating superior performance and reliability while confirming the importance of proper gating mechanisms for practical financial applications.

title: QueryMarket: Cost-Aware Online Active Learning in Data Markets
comment: 10 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
summary: Data acquisition is a major bottleneck for learning in real-time streams: analysts must decide on the fly which labels to purchase while respecting a rolling budget. However, existing online active learning rarely unifies pricing, information gain, and rolling budget constraints under concept drift. We introduce QueryMarket, a market-inspired framework that queries each incoming data point based on its estimated utility to the model and its price. Within this framework, we propose OVBAL (online variance-based active learning), which integrates data pricing with information-driven selection by estimating each sample's marginal utility via a D-optimality criterion with exponential forgetting and executing cost-aware purchases under rolling budget constraints. OVBAL yields a simple, fully online decision rule that adapts to nonstationary streams and heterogeneous label costs. Experiments on synthetic data and a real-world solar power generation forecasting task show that OVBAL is particularly effective under seller-centric pricing and yields a more favorable long-run error-cost trade-off in the real-world task under both pricing schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces QueryMarket, a framework for real-time data acquisition based on sample utility and cost, and proposes OVBAL, an online, budget-aware algorithm for nonstationary streams.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of real-time data acquisition with rolling budget constraints under concept drift.
AI.method: Develop QueryMarket framework and propose OVBAL algorithm using D-optimality criteria, exponential forgetting, and cost-aware purchases.
AI.result: Experiments show OVBAL outperforms alternatives, particularly under seller-centric pricing, improving error-cost trade-offs in real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: OVBAL effectively balances cost and information in online active learning for dynamic, nonstationary environments.

title: High-Fidelity 3D Geometric Reconstruction of Pelvic Organs from MRI: A Hybrid Deep Learning and Iterative Optimization Approach
summary: Patient-specific 3D reconstruction of pelvic organ geometry from MRI is important for pelvic floor modeling and downstream patient-specific analysis. However, while previous studies have focused primarily on either image segmentation or downstream use of 3D models, the reconstruction of high-fidelity, high-quality geometries remains labor-intensive and poorly standardized. The study introduced a hybrid deformable shape modeling framework that integrates deep learning prediction with iterative optimization for the reconstruction of the bladder, uterus, and rectum. The framework consists of three core components: a geometry-aware multi-level deep learning architecture that preserves topological consistency of pelvic organs; a two-stage amortized optimization training strategy that balances global shape capture and local surface refinement; and a holistic synergy mechanism--where iterative optimization provides supervision for deep learning during the training phase, and during inference, deep learning rapidly predicts the global organ morphology, followed by iterative optimization to refine local surfaces and mesh quality. This framework demonstrated marked superiority in geometric fidelity than current mainstream deep learning-based organ reconstruction models. For individual anatomical structures, the reconstructed 3D geometries for the bladder, rectum, and uterus achieved significantly lower Chamfer Distance values and higher Dice Similarity Coefficient scores. In addition, while maintaining high computational efficiency, the proposed architecture yielded superior overall volumetric mesh quality. At the patient level, the framework achieved higher mean values for the 10 worst elements for both minSICN and minSIGE compared to traditional geometric post-processing algorithms.

AI.tldr: This study introduces a hybrid framework combining deep learning and optimization for efficiently and accurately reconstructing high-quality 3D geometries of pelvic organs from MRI.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing accurate 3D geometries of pelvic organs from MRI is vital for patient-specific analysis and pelvic floor modeling, but current methods are labor-intensive and lack standardization.
AI.method: A framework combining multi-level deep learning and iterative optimization was developed, featuring geometry-aware structure, two-stage training for global and local accuracy, and synergistic learning between deep learning and optimization.
AI.result: The proposed model achieved superior fidelity for bladder, rectum, and uterus reconstruction, with improved Chamfer Distance and Dice Similarity Coefficient metrics, high mesh quality, and efficient computational performance.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid framework enhances geometric fidelity and computational efficiency, offering a significant advancement in patient-specific pelvic organ 3D modeling.

title: KFTD: Koopman-Fourier Time-Differentiable Network for Continuous Ocean Spatiotemporal Forecasting
summary: Accurate oceanic forecasting is critical for climate monitoring and disaster early warning. However, ocean spatiotemporal forecasting encounters the double challenges of modeling complex dynamical systems and ensuring computational efficiency. We present Koopman Fourier Time-Differentiable (KFTD) Network, a time continuous twostage paradigm that decouples interpolation from prediction to achieve efficient and scalable spatiotemporal modeling. We map complex nonlinear dynamics into the Koopman linear space and exploit Fourier analysis to enable continuous time interpolation at arbitrary sub-steps. A lightweight residual network consumes the high fidelity intermediate states to yield the final forecast. Unlike diffusion models, KFTD eliminates multi step noise sampling and directly evolves the system in continuous time, yielding a 4 computational speedup. We further introduce a DPP Loss that supports arbitrary PDE constraints in an endtoend manner, breaking the physical consistency bottleneck of pure data-driven approaches. Empirical results on four ocean datasets confirm that our continuous time framework reduces MSE by an average of 5.6% (up to 12.7% for SST) and improves efficiency over MCVD by 76.25%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Koopman Fourier Time-Differentiable (KFTD) Network for efficient and scalable oceanic spatiotemporal forecasting, achieving improved accuracy and computational speed.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges in oceanic forecasting of modeling complex dynamics while maintaining computational efficiency, essential for climate monitoring and disaster early warning.
AI.method: The authors propose the KFTD Network, which maps nonlinear dynamics into a Koopman linear space, uses Fourier analysis for continuous time interpolation, and a residual network for accurate final forecasts. A novel DPP Loss is introduced to incorporate physical constraints seamlessly.
AI.result: KFTD achieves a 4x computational speedup over diffusion models, reduces MSE by 5.6% on average (up to 12.7% for SST), and improves efficiency by 76.25% compared to MCVD.
AI.conclusion: KFTD demonstrates the potential to enable accurate, scalable, and physically consistent oceanic spatiotemporal forecasting, contributing to improved climate monitoring and disaster early warning.

title: No-Free-Fairness: Fundamental Limits and Trade-offs in Learning Systems
summary: In this paper, we establish a set of theoretical impossibility results, termed the No-Free-Fairness theorems, that identify three fundamental sources of disparity in learning systems. First, we show that when a task exhibits irreducible cost on a subgroup, any decision rule must trade off overall performance with disparity, yielding an inherent fairness--cost frontier. Second, we prove that even in ideal, noise-free settings where a perfectly fair and accurate solution exists, finite-sample learning alone induces nontrivial subgroup disparity, ruling out distribution-free fairness guarantees. More seriously, enforcing strict relative fairness creates a statistical bottleneck: achieving low cost may require exponentially many samples. Third, we show that limitations of the model class can independently induce disparity: if the model cannot represent accurate solutions for a subgroup, fairness remains unattainable regardless of data or training procedure. Overall, these results demonstrate that unfairness is not solely a consequence of biased data or suboptimal optimization, but arises from the intrinsic structure of decision problems, the constraints of finite data, and the expressivity of models. Our framework applies broadly beyond standard supervised learning, and suggests that achieving fairness requires explicit trade-offs and should be treated as a core design consideration.

AI.tldr: The paper presents 'No-Free-Fairness' theorems, identifying three intrinsic sources of disparity in learning systems: irreducible task costs, finite-sample learning limitations, and model class constraints.
AI.motivation: To understand fundamental sources of unfairness in learning systems and demonstrate how disparity arises intrinsically, beyond biased data or suboptimal optimization.
AI.method: The authors conduct a theoretical analysis to formulate and prove the No-Free-Fairness theorems, exploring fairness-cost trade-offs, finite-sample limitations, and model constraints.
AI.result: They establish that unfairness can stem from irreducible subgroup costs, learning limitations in finite-sample regimes, and insufficient model expressivity, ruling out universal fairness guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Achieving fairness is an inherently challenging task requiring explicit trade-offs and must be a core design consideration due to the intrinsic structure of learning problems and data constraints.

title: A Quantitative Analysis of Multimodal Biomarkers in Alzheimer's Disease
summary: Despite increasing adoption of multimodal approaches in Alzheimer's Disease (AD) research -- aimed at integrating molecular, structural, clinical, and genetic biomarkers to enhance disease characterization -- the relationships among these modalities remain poorly understood. A systematic analysis of their dynamic interaction is essential for improving disease modeling, identifying redundant assessments, and reducing patient burden and acquisition costs. In this paper, we present a quantitative analysis of multimodal AD biomarkers by integrating tau-PET, structural MRI, cognitive scores (MMSE and CDR), and APOE4 data from 789 subjects drawn from the ADNI dataset. In our analyses, we (A) quantify cross-modal mutual information and explained variance to assess redundancy and predictive dependencies; (B) examine associations between tau topologies and structural atrophy across brain regions to select informative ROIs; (C) perform a statistical decomposition of the tau-cognition association into atrophy-related and atrophy-independent components; (D) and identify a dominant neurodegenerative trajectory that aligns with cognitive decline. This study provides a systematic characterization of cross-modal relationships, improving the interpretability and selection of biomarkers in AD. Code is publicly available at: https://github.com/antonioscardace/Multimodal-AD.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically analyzes relationships among multimodal biomarkers for Alzheimer's Disease to improve disease modeling and identify redundancies.
AI.motivation: To better understand the relationships between molecular, structural, clinical, and genetic biomarkers in Alzheimer's Disease to improve modeling and reduce costs.
AI.method: Quantitative analysis of tau-PET, structural MRI, cognitive scores, and APOE4 data from 789 subjects; measuring cross-modal mutual information, examining tau-atrophy associations, and identifying dominant neurodegenerative trajectories.
AI.result: The study revealed redundancies, predictive dependencies, associations between tau and atrophy, and a main neurodegenerative pathway linked to cognitive decline.
AI.conclusion: The findings enhance the understanding of multimodal biomarker interactions, facilitating better selection and interpretation for Alzheimer's Disease research.

title: Conservation Laws for Modern Neural Architectures
comment: Published at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Understanding gradient descent dynamics is key to explaining the success of over-parameterized models, where implicit bias manifests through conservation laws in gradient flow. While such laws are well understood for linear and ReLU networks, they remain largely unexplored for modern architectures. This work develops a unified framework to characterize conservation laws for contemporary models, including feedforward networks with GELU, SiLU, and SwiGLU activations, multihead attention with sinusoidal and rotary positional encodings, and Mixture-of-Experts architectures under diverse gating designs. Our theoretical findings are supported by experiments that validate the predicted invariants.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a framework to identify conservation laws in advanced neural networks, validating these findings with experiments.
AI.motivation: To explain the success of over-parameterized models and understand implicit bias through gradient descent dynamics in contemporary architectures.
AI.method: Unified theoretical framework to characterize conservation laws for advanced model types, supported by experimental validation.
AI.result: Identified conservation laws for models like GELU, SiLU, SwiGLU, multihead attention, and Mixture-of-Experts, and experimentally validated predicted invariants.
AI.conclusion: Gradient descent dynamics exhibit conservation laws across diverse neural network architectures, enriching understanding of implicit bias in modern models.

title: Revisiting Structural Dependency in Autoregressive Multi-Task Table Recognition via Order-Independent Cell-Level Representations
summary: Multi-task table recognition jointly addresses table structure prediction, cell localization, and cell content recognition within a unified framework. Existing approaches often rely on autoregressive decoders to generate table structures and reuse their hidden states for cell localization and content recognition. This autoregressive generation process can make cell representations order-dependent, degrading global consistency across cells. This paper proposes a structural refinement module that produces order-independent cell features through non-causal attention. This design enables parallel inference of cell contents while conditioning each cell on global context encoded in the refined features. Experiments on two large datasets demonstrate consistent gains in cell localization and end-to-end recognition, while reducing overall inference time by around threefold.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a structural refinement module that addresses table structure prediction, cell localization, and cell content recognition with improved accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing methods relying on autoregressive decoders often result in order-dependent cell representations, affecting the global consistency of table recognition.
AI.method: The paper proposes a structural refinement module using non-causal attention to ensure order-independent cell features, enabling parallel inference conditioned on globally refined features.
AI.result: Experiments on large datasets show improved performance in cell localization and end-to-end table recognition with a threefold reduction in inference time.
AI.conclusion: The structural refinement module enhances global consistency, efficiency, and accuracy of table recognition tasks.

title: Functional Equivalence in Attention: A Comprehensive Study with Applications to Linear Mode Connectivity
comment: Published at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Neural network parameter spaces are inherently non-injective, as distinct parameter configurations can realize identical functions through functional equivalence. While this symmetry is well understood in classical fully connected and convolutional models, it becomes substantially more intricate in modern attention-based architectures. Existing analyses of multihead attention have largely focused on the vanilla formulation, overlooking positional encodings that fundamentally reshape architectural symmetries. In this work, we provide a formal study of functional equivalence in Transformers with positional encodings. Focusing on the two most widely used variants--sinusoidal and rotary positional encodings (RoPE)--we show that sinusoidal encodings preserve the equivalence structure of vanilla attention, whereas rotary encodings significantly reduce the symmetry group, thereby enhancing expressivity. This offers a principled explanation for the growing prominence of RoPE in practice. We further examine how positional encodings affect linear mode connectivity, and through an alignment algorithm, empirically demonstrate that the presence and variability of connectivity across Transformer settings crucially depend on the positional encoding.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the role of positional encodings in Transformers, analyzing their impact on functional equivalence and symmetry. It focuses particularly on sinusoidal and rotary positional encodings, showing that RoPE reduces architectural symmetry and enhances expressivity, which explains its practical popularity.
AI.motivation: To formally understand how positional encodings affect functional equivalence and symmetry in Transformer architectures, especially given their overlooked role in reshaping architectural properties.
AI.method: The study conducts a theoretical analysis of functional equivalence in Transformers with different positional encodings, focusing on sinusoidal vs. rotary. Additionally, it uses an alignment algorithm to empirically assess how positional encoding impacts linear mode connectivity.
AI.result: The research finds that sinusoidal encodings retain vanilla attention's equivalence structure, while rotary encodings significantly reduce symmetry, increasing expressivity. Empirical results also highlight positional encoding's crucial influence on linear mode connectivity in Transformers.
AI.conclusion: Rotary positional encodings (RoPE) improve Transformer expressivity by reducing symmetry, offering a theoretical basis for their practical effectiveness and growing adoption. Positional encodings also impact connectivity properties in these models.

title: MoonSplat: Monocular Online Gaussian Splatting with Sim(3) Global Optimization
summary: Online 3D reconstruction from monocular image sequences is a challenging and ongoing research topic. 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), leveraging its high-quality real-time rendering capability, empowers online 3D reconstruction to represent dense scenes with enhanced expressiveness, and thus holds great promise for a wide range of applications such as robotics and AR/VR. However, existing online 3DGS methods still suffer from some key challenges: fragile camera pose estimation due to the lack of global optimization, and low optimization efficiency in large-scale or long-sequence scenarios. To address these issues, we propose a robust and efficient online voxelized 3DGS reconstruction framework integrated with global $\text{Sim}(3)$ optimization, which enables reliable camera tracking and efficient global loop closure for both camera poses and voxelized 3DGS. To accelerate the convergence of the voxelized 3DGS, we further introduce a color residual learning strategy, which not only boosts optimization speed but also enhances rendering quality. Extensive experiments on diverse indoor and outdoor datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both camera pose estimation accuracy and rendering quality, while retaining real-time efficiency. Additionally, we develop and deploy a real-world UAV-based active reconstruction system grounded on our proposed method, validating its robustness and generalizability for practical online 3D reconstruction tasks. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/TrickyGo/MoonSplat.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a robust and efficient online voxelized 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) framework that enhances reliability in camera pose estimation, rendering quality, and real-time efficiency for practical 3D reconstruction tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing online 3DGS methods face challenges in reliable camera pose estimation and optimization efficiency, particularly in large-scale or long-sequence scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose a voxelized 3DGS framework integrating global Sim(3) optimization for improved camera tracking and loop closure, along with a color residual learning strategy to boost optimization speed and rendering quality.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both camera pose estimation accuracy and rendering quality while maintaining real-time efficiency, as shown in experiments on various indoor and outdoor datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework is validated for robustness and generalizability in real-world applications via a UAV-based active reconstruction system, making it suitable for practical online 3D reconstruction tasks.

title: CMIP-Forge: An Agentic System that Retrieves, Computes, and Self-Reviews Climate Science
comment: 28 pages, 9 figures. Code available at https://github.com/CliDyn/cmip6_gpt
summary: The Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) has generated thousands of peer-reviewed publications documenting model configurations, evaluation procedures, emergent constraints, and projection uncertainties. As the community transitions toward CMIP7, efficiently extracting and operationalizing this unstructured knowledge alongside live data analysis represents a critical bottleneck. Here we present CMIP-Forge, a hybrid retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and autonomous analysis system that bridges the gap between scientific literature and Earth System Grid Federation (ESGF) data archives. The system pairs a curated corpus of 6,581 CMIP6-related open-access publications (101,828 indexed chunks) with an agentic pipeline in which a tool-augmented worker plans and executes Python workflows over live climate data, while a panel of independent reviewer models audits its methodology end to end. CMIP-Forge introduces a multi-layered Defense-in-Depth architecture that enforces physical and methodological invariants through executable mechanisms: Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) static analysis, audited scientific primitives, and an autonomous adversarial peer-review protocol. We demonstrate the system's capabilities through end-to-end autonomous research pipelines spanning atmospheric teleconnections, ocean dynamics, regional extremes, and global warming projections. An agentic analysis system grounded in peer-reviewed literature, constrained by automated code guardrails, and audited by an independent adversarial review loop can complete complex climate-research workflows autonomously. The same experiments expose concrete failure modes of the review loop (sycophantic regression, REVISE verdicts that are never resolved, and the submission of stub code for review), each diagnosable from the immutable telemetry and provenance record released with the article.

AI.tldr: CMIP-Forge is a system designed to streamline working with CMIP6 climate model data and literature by combining retrieval-augmented generation and autonomous tools for data analysis and review.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses a critical challenge in transitioning from CMIP6 to CMIP7: efficiently integrating and operationalizing vast amounts of unstructured scientific literature with live data analysis.
AI.method: CMIP-Forge employs a hybrid retrieval-augmented generation system complemented by an agent-based pipeline. It uses archived CMIP6 literature, static analysis safeguards, scientific primitives, and adversarial peer-review protocols to ensure robust research workflows.
AI.result: The system successfully demonstrated autonomous capabilities in conducting complex climate research tasks. However, it also revealed key failure points, such as unresolved review feedback and susceptibility to flawed submissions like stub code.
AI.conclusion: CMIP-Forge represents a step forward in autonomous climate research by effectively integrating literature retrieval and automated analysis. Its design ensures rigorous auditing, but the need to address practical challenges in its review mechanism is underscored.

title: From Drift to Coherence: Stabilizing Beliefs in LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are often hypothesized to perform implicit Bayesian inference, yet a key coherence condition, the martingale property of predictive beliefs, has been shown to fail in controlled synthetic in-context learning settings. We revisit this question in a more typical usage regime: generic multiple-choice question answering. Exploiting the discrete answer space, we compute exact predictive distributions and study belief dynamics induced by autoregressive answer resampling. We introduce prompted predictive resampling (PPR), where an LLM generates a sequence of answers to the same question. Empirically, PPR reveals early-stage belief drift, indicating martingale violations. However, after sufficient resampling steps, the belief process self-stabilizes and converges to a coherent predictive distribution. Based on this observation, we further propose (i) a seed-answer prompting strategy to accelerate stabilization, and (ii) a self-consistency loss that amortizes early-stage drift into the model via fine-tuning. Experiments on multiple-choice QA benchmarks show that our methods substantially reduce belief drift and improve predictive coherence without sacrificing accuracy.

AI.tldr: LLMs show belief drift in predictive distributions during question answering, but self-stabilize after several resampling steps. Proposed techniques improve predictive coherence.
AI.motivation: To understand and improve the coherence of belief dynamics in LLMs during common use cases like question answering.
AI.method: Introduced PPR for studying belief dynamics, along with seed-answer prompting and self-consistency loss to mitigate belief drift.
AI.result: Empirical results reveal early belief drift and eventual stabilization in LLMs, and proposed methods reduce drift while maintaining accuracy.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can achieve better predictive coherence for question answering through innovative belief stabilization strategies.

title: Plug-and-Adapt: Multimodal Coreference Resolution at First Sight with a Pretrained Alignment Model
summary: Visual information helps resolve ambiguity in coreference resolution, leading to notable performance gains. However, existing Multi-modal Coreference Resolution (MCR) methods require training with (partially) annotated data from the target dataset before they can be applied, preventing their direct usability and raising concerns about generalization. While Vision-Language Large Models (VLLMs) with billions of parameters offer promising zero-shot capabilities, they remain largely inaccessible. Their massive size limits deployability, and many are only accessible through paid APIs. In this paper, we propose a plug-and-adapt method that strategically adapts a carefully pre-trained \emph{alignment model} for immediate use in MCR tasks, designed to eliminate the need for training on scarce benchmark datasets or relying on resource-intensive VLLMs. Specifically, we first pre-train a fine-grained alignment model between textual and visual contextual information using vision-language alignment datasets. We then repurpose the alignment model to MCR through similarity aggregation by fusing visual and categorical cues with evidence theory, thereby enhancing effectiveness. Experiments on the Coreference Image Narratives (CIN) benchmark dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, achieving a 5.31\% and 2.12\% improvement in CoNLL F1 over SOTA dedicated methods and popular VLLMs, respectively. We further evaluate our method on a masked CIN dataset for robustness testing and on a specially constructed VCR-MCR dataset for generalization assessment, with results confirming both capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a plug-and-adapt method for Multi-modal Coreference Resolution (MCR) that eliminates the need for training on specific datasets or using large vision-language models (VLLMs), achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing MCR methods that depend on training with annotated datasets or rely on resource-intensive, inaccessible VLLMs, this work aims to create a lightweight, adaptable solution.
AI.method: The authors pre-train a fine-grained alignment model for text-image contextual alignment using vision-language datasets. This model is adapted for MCR by aggregating similarity using evidence theory, effectively integrating visual and categorical cues.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves a 5.31% and 2.12% improvement in CoNLL F1 on the CIN dataset over state-of-the-art MCR methods and large VLLMs. Robustness and generalization tests on specialized datasets also affirm its performance.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that their plug-and-adapt approach offers an effective, lightweight alternative for MCR tasks, outperforming existing methods while addressing accessibility and scalability issues in VLLMs.

title: Comprehensive pKa Data Augmentation from Limited Real Data through an Engineered Models-Quantum Framework
summary: Proton dissociation constants (pKa) are critical for functional molecule discovery and molecular modeling. Building on iBonD, the largest experimental pKa database established, we and other researchers have developed several methods including machine-learning-based empirical prediction and high-accuracy energy calculations. Despite this foundation, the rapid augmentation of high-quality pKa data remains fundamentally constrained. As part of this work, we performed large-scale regression-based pKa prediction on unlabeled molecular datasets using a collection of extensively optimized machine-learning models. The results indicate that, since the feature distributions of unlabeled molecular datasets, the pKa data distribution approximates normality, with extreme scarcity of tail-region samples. Although such augmentation is highly valuable for improving overall data availability and predictive modeling, it remains insufficient for efficiently discovering molecules with broad-spectrum pKa properties. To address this, we explore the targeted generation of molecules with sparse pKa properties from the vast chemical space. Given that traditional continuous latent space VAE-RNN methods for molecular generation suffer from insufficient stability and fail to demonstrate clear advantages in complementing sparse data, we design and implement a quantum-assisted sparse-pKa molecular generation. Feasibility is validated on a simulated quantum annealer, and superior extreme-value sampling is further achieved on physical coherent Ising machines (CIMs). (to be continued)

AI.tldr: The paper discusses enhancing pKa data through quantum-assisted molecular generation, surpassing traditional VAE-RNN approaches in sparse pKa sampling.
AI.motivation: Enable efficient discovery and modeling of molecules with broad-spectrum or rare pKa properties to address current data limitations.
AI.method: Develop and optimize quantum-assisted molecular generation using simulated quantum annealers and coherent Ising machines.
AI.result: Validated feasibility of quantum-assisted approaches and achieved superior sampling of molecules with rare pKa properties.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-assisted methodologies demonstrate significant advantages in generating sparse-pKa molecules, overcoming limitations of traditional approaches.

title: Meta-classification of one-class classification models using ranking correlation and nearest neighbor
summary: Machine Learning (ML) techniques have been applied to various problems. However, applying ML to ML models is an unexplored direction. For this purpose, this paper considers a meta-classification of one-class classification (OCC) models, because all ML models could be approximated as OCC models. The proposal represents OCC models as normality rankings and classifies them using nearest-neighbor and ranking-correlation metrics. The experiment classifies OCC models, where classes correspond to training datasets, algorithms, and hyperparameters. The proposal achieves high accuracy when class labels are datasets. Moreover, it can classify algorithms when the training datasets contain the same class. In addition, the discussion highlights that the classification of OCC models is essentially the classification of datasets that treats multiple samples as a single input. The experiment demonstrates the classification of datasets using sleeping records. The proposed method can provide a unified solution for classifying OCC models, datasets, and rankings. Source code is uploaded to the public repository https://github.com/ToshiHayashi/ClassOCC.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces meta-classification for one-class classification (OCC) models, demonstrating how ML can classify ML models themselves.
AI.motivation: To explore the unexplored direction of applying machine learning to classify ML models, specifically one-class classification (OCC) models.
AI.method: The method involves representing OCC models as normality rankings and employing nearest-neighbor and ranking-correlation metrics for classification. It focuses on datasets, algorithms, and hyperparameters.
AI.result: The proposal achieved high accuracy in classifying OCC models by datasets, and it demonstrated capabilities in classifying algorithms and datasets that contain the same classes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method introduces a unified approach to classify OCC models, datasets, and rankings, providing new insights into ML applications. A practical example of its utility is presented via sleeping record datasets.

title: MLLMs Get It Right, Then Get It Wrong: Tracing and Correcting Late-Layer Textual Bias
summary: When vision contradicts text, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) consistently favor text, even when images provide clear evidence otherwise. This bias poses risks for applications requiring visual grounding, yet its cause remains unclear. In this paper, we uncover a surprising finding: models often get it right initially, forming correct vision-based predictions in their intermediate layers, before changing their minds and favoring text in the final output. We call this "late-layer textual override". The visual information is encoded, it simply does not survive to the output. More intriguingly, we find that how predictions change reveals whether they're correct: 85% of failures shift toward text, while 89% of successes shift toward vision. This directional signature enables a simple but powerful intervention: when we detect a confident visual prediction being suppressed, we restore it. We propose CALRD (Conflict-Aware Layer Reference Decoding), a training-free method that recovers overridden predictions at inference time. Experiments across five MLLMs of varying architectures demonstrate up to 9.4% absolute improvements on conflict benchmarks while largely preserving standard performance, without training or external knowledge. It recovers what the model already knew but failed to preserve.

AI.tldr: The study reveals a bias in multimodal large language models where textual information overrides visual evidence, even when visual cues clearly contradict the text. A training-free method is proposed to mitigate this issue, leading to improved performance.
AI.motivation: To address the risk posed by multimodal language models favoring text over visually grounded evidence, which undermines their reliability in visual conflict scenarios.
AI.method: The authors analyze intermediate and late-layer processing in MLLMs and propose a training-free intervention method called CALRD (Conflict-Aware Layer Reference Decoding) to restore suppressed visual predictions during inference.
AI.result: Experiments on five different MLLMs across conflict benchmarks reveal absolute performance improvements of up to 9.4% while maintaining standard performance.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight and address the textual bias in MLLMs, demonstrating that suppressed visual predictions can be successfully recovered, enhancing robustness without additional training or external inputs.

title: AnchorKV: Safety-Aware KV Cache Compression via Soft Penalty with a Refusal Anchor
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) outperform earlier architectures on generative inference and long-context tasks, but their large size introduces significant challenges in memory usage, energy cost, and on-device deployment. Since scaling pre-trained language models improves downstream capability \cite{zhao2023survey}, the key-value (KV) cache becomes a dominant inference bottleneck. Recent KV cache compression methods \cite{jo2025fastkv,li2024snapkv,zhou2024dynamickv} reduce this cost by retaining only a subset of attention-relevant tokens. However, while these approaches preserve accuracy on benign workloads, their compression policies either fail to defend against jailbreak attacks \cite{jiang2024robustkv} or degrade safety alignment under aggressive eviction.
  We propose AnchorKV, a drop-in modification to KV cache compression that biases token retention scores away from directions in key space associated with harmful prompts. AnchorKV constructs an offline safety anchor by adapting a difference-of-means representation engineering approach \cite{arditi2024refusal,zou2023representation} to the layer-specific key projection space used in KV caching. Based on this anchor, a soft penalty token selection rule trades a small amount of utility for substantially improved safety alignment, while reducing to the original compressor when the penalty is zero.

AI.tldr: AnchorKV enhances KV cache compression in large language models for better safety alignment, addressing challenges posed by jailbreak attacks and aggressive evictions.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in KV cache compression for LLMs, such as memory, energy, and safety alignment, especially under adversarial conditions.
AI.method: Introduce AnchorKV, a token retention policy that biases retention scores using an offline safety anchor derived through a difference-of-means representation engineering method.
AI.result: Improved safety alignment while retaining utility, with protection against harmful prompt jailbreak attacks, achieving better compression efficacy.
AI.conclusion: AnchorKV serves as a drop-in solution for KV cache compression, balancing compression efficiency with enhanced safety.

title: Toward Accessible Psychotherapy Training Using AI-Driven Interactive Patient Avatars
summary: Training psychotherapists in evidence-based interventions such as Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) requires repeated practice with meaningful feedback, yet opportunities for safe, standardized training are limited by ethical, logistical, and resource constraints. We introduce a system designed to support ACT-oriented psychotherapy training through spoken dialogue with an embodied virtual patient. The system uses large language models to simulate patient behavior conditioned on profiles derived from real therapy sessions and configurable clinical scenarios, while a separate automated evaluator provides turn-by-turn feedback on therapist responses based on established ACT fidelity criteria. Rather than aiming to replace supervision, the system is intended to support deliberate practice by enabling experimentation, reflection, and immediate feedback in low-risk settings. Expert evaluation with practicing psychologists confirmed high realism in patient behavior and demonstrated that immediate turn-by-turn ACT feedback increased therapists' awareness of intervention choices and enabled effective experimentation with alternative responses. Quantitative evaluation across 49 therapy transcripts identified GPT-4o-mini as the optimal feedback model, achieving the lowest mean absolute error (MAE = 6.12) in replicating human supervisor ACT fidelity ratings with statistically significant agreement. This work demonstrates the potential of fidelity-aware simulated patients as a scalable complement to psychotherapy training.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a system for training psychotherapists using a virtual patient simulation powered by large language models, providing real-time feedback on therapist responses based on ACT therapy criteria.
AI.motivation: The training of psychotherapists in evidence-based approaches like ACT is restricted by ethical, logistical, and resource issues, limiting opportunities for safe, standardized, and meaningful practice.
AI.method: The authors developed a system that employs large language models to simulate patient behavior, drawing on clinical scenarios and real therapy session profiles, while an automated evaluator provides immediate feedback based on ACT fidelity criteria.
AI.result: Expert evaluations confirmed that the virtual patient demonstrated realistic behavior and effective feedback improved therapists' intervention awareness. The GPT-4o-mini model showed the best performance in mimicking human feedback with the lowest error (6.12 MAE).
AI.conclusion: The system represents a scalable and effective complement to psychotherapy training by facilitating deliberate practice, experimentation, and reflection in low-risk environments.

title: Beyond Visual Cues: CoT-Enhanced Reasoning for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Semi-supervised medical image segmentation has emerged as a dominant research problem in medical image analysis, mitigating annotation scarcity by leveraging consistency regularization on unlabeled data. However, existing approaches operate predominantly via visual pattern matching, relying heavily on pixel-level similarities. This visual-centric dependency often falters in clinical scenarios characterized by the visual-semantic mismatch, where visually similar lesions warrant distinct diagnostic conclusions, thus failing to capture the underlying diagnostic logic used by experts. To address this, we move beyond visual cues and propose CERS (CoT-Enhanced Reasoning Segmentation), a framework that integrates Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to distinguish pathologically distinct cases. Specifically, we construct a knowledge pool enriched with linguistic reasoning descriptions generated by large language models (LLMs). A semantic-aware reference selection strategy is introduced to identify historical evidence, filtering candidates first by morphology, and then refining them via CoT consistency to eliminate hard negatives. Furthermore, a multi-scale coordinate attention module (MCAM) is designed to effectively fuse this reasoning-derived context into the decoding process. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of CERS against state-of-the-art approaches, particularly in resolving boundary ambiguities and semantic inconsistencies. The code is available at https://github.com/cymasuna/CERS.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel framework called CERS (CoT-Enhanced Reasoning Segmentation) for semi-supervised medical image segmentation that integrates Chain-of-Thought reasoning to address visual-semantic mismatches in medical imaging tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations of existing semi-supervised segmentation methods, which rely heavily on visual similarity and fail in clinical scenarios involving visual-semantic mismatches.
AI.method: The CERS framework uses a knowledge pool of linguistic reasoning from large language models and a semantic-aware reference selection strategy, along with a multi-scale coordinate attention module (MCAM) for integrating reasoning context into image segmentation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that CERS outperforms state-of-the-art methods, especially in resolving boundary ambiguities and addressing semantic inconsistencies in segmentation tasks.
AI.conclusion: CERS demonstrates the effectiveness of incorporating reasoning and semantic understanding into medical image segmentation, providing a promising direction for handling complex clinical scenarios.

title: Monotonic Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks: A Theoretical and Empirical Study of Monotonicity as an Inductive Bias
summary: Monotonicity has been a long-running architectural inductive bias for neural networks, motivated by tabular, scientific, and economic settings where outputs are known to respond monotonically to certain inputs. Existing approaches are MLP- or flow-based and lack per-edge functional transparency; the only Kolmogorov--Arnold Network (KAN) variant with monotonicity, MonoKAN, enforces the constraint only on a restricted parameter subset and requires a projection-style training procedure. We close this gap with \textbf{MKAN}, a KAN with hard monotonicity guaranteed for \emph{all} parameter values via exponential reparameterization of B-spline coefficients, positive edge weights, and a monotone base activation.
